topics.title: Materials for MEMS: Silicon - Structure, Orientations, and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT744 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Materials for MEMS: Silicon - Structure, Orientations, and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Silicon (Si)** is a Group IV elemental semiconductor (atomic number **Z = 14**, atomic mass **M = 28.0855 g/mol**) that crystallizes in the **diamond cubic (DC) crystal structure** with a lattice constant of **a = 5.4309 Å (0.54309 nm) at 300 K**. It is the foundational structural and functional material of Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) due to a unique convergence of excellent mechanical strength, tunable electrical properties, mature microfabrication infrastructure, and well-characterized anisotropic chemical behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT744 – Module 1):**
> "Materials for MEMS: Silicon – crystal structure, wafer orientations (Miller indices), mechanical, electrical, thermal, and chemical properties; relevance to bulk and surface micromachining."

The **diamond cubic structure** can be visualized as two inter-penetrating face-centered cubic (FCC) sublattices, offset along the body diagonal by **a/4 (√3 a/4)**, where each Si atom is covalently bonded to **4 nearest neighbors** in a perfect tetrahedral arrangement with a bond length of **2.35 Å** and a bond angle of **109.47°**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a microscopic skyscraper. The "bricks" of this skyscraper must be:
1. **Strong** enough to support moving mechanical parts without bending,
2. **Smart** enough to sense stress, temperature, or motion,
3. **Cheap and abundant** so that millions of identical chips can be mass-produced,
4. **Friendly to sculpting** at the micro-scale using chemical etchants.

Silicon perfectly fits this analogy — it is essentially **"sand (SiO₂) purified and crystallized into a near-perfect mechanical-grade crystal."** The same beach sand that you walk on can be grown into flawless 300 mm diameter single-crystal boules, sliced into wafers thinner than a human hair, and etched into a million working micro-gears or pressure-sensing diaphragms in a single batch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not metals or polymers?** Metals deform plastically (no spring-back) and polymers creep (dimensions drift over time). Silicon behaves as a near-perfect **elastic solid** (no dislocation glide at room temperature) and fractures cleanly — which is why MEMS designers prefer it for high-Q resonators, gyroscopes, and pressure sensors.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Silicon's Dominance in MEMS

| Pillar | Property | MEMS Implication |
|--------|----------|------------------|
| **Mechanical** | High Young's modulus (130–188 GPa), High yield strength (~7 GPa) | Stiff, non-creeping micro-structures |
| **Electrical** | Tunable resistivity (10⁻³ – 10⁴ Ω·cm), piezoresistive | Integrated strain gauges |
| **Thermal** | Low thermal expansion (2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K), High thermal conductivity (150 W/m·K) | Thermal actuators, heat sinks |
| **Chemical** | Anisotropic etching in KOH/TMAH; native oxide SiO₂ | Predictable 3-D sculpting |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Diamond Cubic Crystal Structure — Step-by-Step

1. **Start with FCC lattice** — 8 corner atoms + 6 face-centers → contribute 4 atoms/cell.
2. **Add a second FCC sublattice** displaced by **(a/4, a/4, a/4)** → adds 4 internal atoms.
3. **Total atoms per unit cell** = 4 (FCC) + 4 (internal) = **8 atoms**.
4. **Nearest-neighbor distance** (bond length):
$$d_{NN} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 5.4309}{4} = 2.3517 \text{ Å}$$
5. **Atomic density (N_v)** — number of Si atoms per unit volume:
$$N_v = \frac{8}{a^3} = \frac{8}{(5.4309 \times 10^{-8})^3} = 5.00 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/cm}^3$$
6. **Packing fraction (APF)** — fraction of cell volume occupied by atoms:
$$APF = \frac{8 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{a^3} = \frac{8 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{8}\right)^3}{a^3} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{3}}{16} \approx 0.3401$$
7. **Conclusion:** Silicon is a **loosely packed** crystal (~34% APF) — meaning **66% is empty space**, which enables **interstitial doping** and **anisotropic chemical etching** along preferred crystallographic directions.

### 2.2 Miller Indices — The Language of Crystal Planes

The **Miller indices (h k l)** uniquely identify a family of crystallographic planes. The construction procedure is:
- Identify the **plane intercepts** on the x, y, z axes as multiples of the lattice constant `a`.
- Take the **reciprocals** of these intercepts.
- **Clear fractions** to obtain the smallest integer triplet (h k l).

> [!NOTE]
> **Family notation:** `{hkl}` denotes all symmetry-equivalent planes; `<uvw>` denotes all symmetry-equivalent directions.

### 2.3 Critical Silicon Wafer Orientations for MEMS

| Wafer Surface | Miller Index | Surface Symmetry | Etch Behavior in KOH/TMAH | Typical MEMS Use |
|---------------|--------------|------------------|----------------------------|------------------|
| **(100)** | (100) | 4-fold (square) | Fast etch → **54.74° sidewalls** | Pressure sensors, accelerometers, bulk-micromachined diaphragms |
| **(110)** | (110) | 2-fold (rectangular) | Very fast along ⟨110⟩ → **vertical (90°) sidewalls** | Deep trenches, micro-channels, comb-drives with vertical walls |
| **(111)** | (111) | 6-fold (hexagonal) | Extremely slow (etches ~1000× slower than (100)) | **Etch-stop layer** for precise diaphragm thickness control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **54.74° angle** between the (100) surface and the {111} sidewalls is a direct consequence of the geometry of the diamond cubic lattice and equals **arctan(√2)**. This angle governs the **V-groove** and **pyramidal pit** shapes etched in (100) silicon.

### 2.4 Mechanical Property Anisotropy — Directional Young's Modulus

Silicon is **mechanically anisotropic** despite being a cubic crystal. The directional Young's modulus for the cubic system along direction `[uvw]` is given by the inverse of the directional compliance:

$$\frac{1}{E_{\langle uvw\rangle}} = S_{11} - 2\left(S_{11}-S_{12}-\tfrac{1}{2}S_{44}\right)\left(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2\right)$$

where `l₁, l₂, l₃` are the direction cosines of `[uvw]`, and `S₁₁, S₁₂, S₄₄` are the **compliance constants** in the cubic reference frame.

> Using Si values: `S₁₁ = 7.68 × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹`, `S₁₂ = -2.14 × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹`, `S₄₄ = 12.6 × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹`.

| Direction | Young's Modulus (E) | Numerical Value |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| ⟨100⟩ | `E = 1/S₁₁` | **130.2 GPa** |
| ⟨110⟩ | `E = 2/(S₁₁+S₁₂+S₄₄/2)` | **168.9 GPa** |
| ⟨111⟩ | `E = 1/(S₁₁+2S₁₂+S₄₄/3)` | **187.5 GPa** |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM TIP:** Memorize the entries below — they account for ~70% of numerical questions on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Value (Si) | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|------------|------|
| 1 | Atoms per unit cell (DC) | `N = 8` | **8** | — |
| 2 | Lattice constant | `a` | **5.4309 × 10⁻¹⁰** | m |
| 3 | Atomic density | `N_v = 8/a³` | **5.00 × 10²²** | atoms/cm³ |
| 4 | Nearest-neighbor distance | `d_NN = (√3·a)/4` | **2.3517 × 10⁻¹⁰** | m |
| 5 | Packing fraction (APF) | `π√3 / 16` | **0.3401** | — |
| 6 | Volume of unit cell | `a³` | **1.602 × 10⁻²²** | cm³ |
| 7 | Density (theoretical) | `ρ = 8·M/(N_A·a³)` | **2.329** | g/cm³ |
| 8 | Young's modulus ⟨100⟩ | `1/S₁₁` | **130.2** | GPa |
| 9 | Young's modulus ⟨110⟩ | (derived) | **168.9** | GPa |
| 10 | Young's modulus ⟨111⟩ | (derived) | **187.5** | GPa |
| 11 | Poisson's ratio (avg) | `ν` | **0.28** | — |
| 12 | Yield strength | `σ_y` | **~7.0** | GPa |
| 13 | Fracture toughness | `K_IC` | **0.7 – 0.9** | MPa·√m |
| 14 | Thermal conductivity | `κ` | **150** | W/(m·K) |
| 15 | Thermal expansion (CTE) | `α` | **2.6 × 10⁻⁶** | /K |
| 16 | Bandgap (300 K) | `E_g` | **1.12** | eV |
| 17 | Piezoresistive coeff. (π₄₄) | `π₄₄` | **~138 × 10⁻¹¹** | Pa⁻¹ |
| 18 | Etch angle (100) wafer | `arctan(√2)` | **54.74°** | degrees |
| 19 | Critical resolved shear stress | `τ_CRSS` | **~1.85** | GPa |
| 20 | Native oxide SiO₂ growth | `B/A + t` parabolic | **≈ 1–2 nm** (native) | nm |

> **Note on absolute values:** All entries use physical magnitude; for the linear intercept formula `\vert h \vert + \vert k \vert + \vert l \vert` in Miller index families, write `$\vert h \vert$` outside tables and `$|h|$` is *not* used inside table cells — use plain integers.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bulk micromachining (100 wafers):** Forms pyramidal pits, V-grooves, and diaphragms used in **automotive MAP/BARO pressure sensors** and **inhaler flow sensors**.
- **Anisotropic etch-stop on (111):** Produces sub-micron-precise diaphragms in **piezoresistive pressure sensors** (e.g., Bosch BMP-series).
- **SOI (110) wafers:** Used in **deep reactive ion etching (DRIE)** alternatives to fabricate inertial-grade MEMS gyroscopes with **vertical comb drives**.
- **High Young's modulus ⟨111⟩:** Maximizes mechanical stiffness-to-mass ratio for **resonator Q-factor** in timing references (SiTime, Microchip).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Atomic Density of Silicon

**Objective:** Compute the number of Si atoms per cm³ given the diamond cubic structure.

**Step 1.** Identify contributing atomic sites in a single conventional cubic cell:

$$\text{8 corners} \times \tfrac{1}{8} = 1 \text{ atom}$$

$$\text{6 face-centers} \times \tfrac{1}{2} = 3 \text{ atoms}$$

$$\text{4 fully internal atoms} \times 1 = 4 \text{ atoms}$$

**Step 2.** Total atoms per cell:
$$N_{cell} = 1 + 3 + 4 = 8 \text{ atoms}$$

**Step 3.** Volume of one conventional cell:
$$V_{cell} = a^3 = (5.4309 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm})^3 = 1.6023 \times 10^{-22} \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4.** Atomic density:
$$N_v = \frac{N_{cell}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{8}{1.6023 \times 10^{-22}} = 4.993 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/cm}^3$$

**Step 5.** Rounding to **3 significant figures** (KTU-typical precision):
$$\boxed{N_v \approx 5.00 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/cm}^3}$$

> **Logic check:** Compare with FCC Cu (8.5 × 10²² /cm³) — silicon is **less dense** atomically because the DC structure has larger empty space, consistent with `APF = 0.34` vs FCC `0.74`.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Density of Silicon

**Objective:** Derive `ρ = 8M/(N_A a³)` and evaluate numerically.

**Step 1.** Mass of one unit cell = (number of atoms) × (mass per atom) = `N_cell · M / N_A`.

**Step 2.** Density = mass / volume:
$$\rho = \frac{N_{cell} \cdot M}{N_A \cdot a^3}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute `N_cell = 8`, `M = 28.0855 g/mol`, `N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ /mol`, `a = 5.4309 × 10⁻⁸ cm`:

$$\rho = \frac{8 \times 28.0855}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times (5.4309 \times 10^{-8})^3}$$

**Step 4.** Compute numerator: `8 × 28.0855 = 224.684 g/mol`

**Step 5.** Compute denominator: `6.022 × 10²³ × 1.6023 × 10⁻²² = 96.49 cm³/mol`

**Step 6.** Final value:
$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{224.684}{96.49} = 2.329 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

> **Engineering link:** This is the **exact** value measured for defect-free float-zone silicon and is used as a calibration standard in metrology-grade mass sensors and buoyant-density sensors.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Angle Between (100) Surface and {111} Sidewall

**Objective:** Prove that the sidewall angle of a KOH-etched (100) pit is exactly **54.74°**.

**Step 1.** The (100) surface has a unit outward normal: $\hat{n}_{100} = (1, 0, 0)$.

**Step 2.** Pick a representative {111} plane, e.g., (1, 1, 1) — its normal is $\hat{n}_{111} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1, 1, 1)$.

**Step 3.** Angle between two planes = angle between their normals:
$$\cos\theta = \hat{n}_{100} \cdot \hat{n}_{111} = (1,0,0) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 4.** Solve:
$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356° \approx 54.74°$$

**Step 5.** Equivalently, the sidewall inclination from the (100) plane is:
$$\theta = \arctan(\sqrt{2}) = 54.7356°$$

> **Logic check:** This is *the* canonical angle in silicon bulk micromachining. V-groove fiber-alignment V-grooves, inkjet-nozzle pits, and pyramidal solar-cell texturing all rely on this exact angle.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Directional Young's Modulus for ⟨110⟩

**Objective:** Derive `E⟨110⟩` from the cubic compliance matrix.

**Step 1.** Direction cosines for `[110]`: `l₁ = l₂ = 1/√2`, `l₃ = 0`.

**Step 2.** Compute the squared products:
$$l_1^2 l_2^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}, \quad l_2^2 l_3^2 = 0, \quad l_3^2 l_1^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum l_i^2 l_j^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}$$

**Step 3.** Anisotropy factor:
$$\Gamma = l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2 = 0.25$$

**Step 4.** Substitute into the directional compliance equation:
$$\frac{1}{E_{\langle 110\rangle}} = S_{11} - 2(S_{11} - S_{12} - \tfrac{1}{2}S_{44}) \cdot 0.25$$

**Step 5.** Simplify:
$$\frac{1}{E_{\langle 110\rangle}} = S_{11} - 0.5(S_{11} - S_{12} - \tfrac{1}{2}S_{44}) = 0.5 S_{11} + 0.5 S_{12} + 0.25 S_{44}$$

**Step 6.** Substitute numerical values:
$$\frac{1}{E} = 0.5(7.68) + 0.5(-2.14) + 0.25(12.6) = 3.84 - 1.07 + 3.15 = 5.92 \text{ TPa}^{-1}$$

**Step 7.** Invert:
$$\boxed{E_{\langle 110\rangle} = \frac{1}{5.92 \times 10^{-12}} = 168.9 \text{ GPa}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why Orientation Choice Matters

| Application | Preferred Wafer | Plane Used | Etch Used | Reason |
|-------------|-----------------|------------|-----------|--------|
| Piezoresistive pressure sensor | (100) | (100) surface + {111} sidewalls | KOH anisotropic | Transverse piezoresistive coeff. π₁₂ maximized |
| Gyroscope (in-plane mode) | (110) | (110) surface | DRIE | Vertical (90°) comb-drive sidewalls |
| Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) tip | (100) | Sharpened {111} tip | Wet oxidation sharpening | 54.74° cone with atomic-sharp apex |
| Thin membrane (etch-stop) | (100) w/ B⁺⁺ layer | (100) + p⁺ etch-stop | KOH + electrochemical | Boron-doped layer stops KOH at ~2 µm precision |
| Solar cell texturing | (100) | Random {111} pyramids | KOH + IPA | Reduces reflection from 35% to <10% |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*"Explain the diamond cubic structure of silicon with a neat sketch. Why is it the most preferred material for MEMS applications?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks — key points expected):**

1. **Structure (1.5 marks):** Silicon crystallizes in the **diamond cubic** arrangement. It consists of two interpenetrating FCC sublattices displaced by `(a/4, a/4, a/4)`. Lattice constant `a = 5.43 Å`. Each Si atom has **4 nearest neighbors** in a tetrahedral arrangement with bond length **2.35 Å** and bond angle **109.47°**. There are **8 atoms per unit cell**.

2. **MEMS preference (1.5 marks):** Silicon offers (i) **high Young's modulus** (130–188 GPa) giving excellent stiffness-to-mass ratio, (ii) **tunable electrical resistivity** with the **piezoresistive effect** enabling integrated strain gauges, (iii) **anisotropic chemical etching** allowing precise 3-D sculpting, and (iv) **CMOS compatibility** enabling monolithic integration with readout circuitry.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*"Differentiate between (100), (110), and (111) silicon wafer orientations with respect to their anisotropic etching behavior."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular key):**

| Feature | (100) | (110) | (111) |
|---------|-------|-------|-------|
| **Surface symmetry** | 4-fold | 2-fold | 6-fold |
| **Etch rate in KOH** | Fast | Fastest | ~1000× slower |
| **Etch pit shape** | Square pyramid (54.74° walls) | Rectangular trench | Hexagonal shallow pit |
| **Sidewall plane** | {111} | {111} & vertical | — |
| **MEMS application** | Pressure sensors, diaphragms | Vertical comb drives | Etch-stop layer |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall (Part A):** Students often confuse **Miller index of plane vs. direction** notation — `(hkl)` denotes a plane, `[uvw]` a direction. One mark is routinely lost for this mix-up.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** "Module-1 has internal choice; the student may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B."

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the diamond cubic crystal structure of silicon. Calculate the **atomic density**, **packing fraction**, and **theoretical density** of silicon. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **Miller indices** of the (100), (110), and (111) planes. Show that the angle between the (100) surface and a {111} sidewall in anisotropic KOH etching is **54.74°**. Discuss why this angle is critical in bulk micromachining. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Structure description:**
Silicon has a diamond cubic structure formed by **two interpenetrating FCC sublattices** displaced by `(a/4, a/4, a/4)`. The conventional cell contains:
- 8 corner atoms × 1/8 = 1
- 6 face-centered atoms × 1/2 = 3
- 4 fully internal atoms = 4
- **Total = 8 atoms/cell** with `a = 5.43 Å`.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Atomic density:**
$$N_v = \frac{8}{a^3} = \frac{8}{(5.43 \times 10^{-8})^3} = 5.00 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/cm}^3$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Packing fraction:**
With nearest-neighbor radius `r = (√3·a)/8`:
$$APF = \frac{8 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{a^3} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{3}}{16} = 0.34 \;(34.01\%)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Theoretical density:**
$$\rho = \frac{8 M}{N_A a^3} = \frac{8 \times 28.0855}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.6023 \times 10^{-22}} = 2.329 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Miller index definitions:**
For plane `(hkl)`, intercepts on x, y, z axes are `a/h, a/k, a/l`. For (100): intercepts `(a, ∞, ∞)`. For (110): `(a, a, ∞)`. For (111): `(a, a, a)`.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Derivation of 54.74°:**
- Surface normal (100) = `(1, 0, 0)`.
- {111} plane normal e.g. (1,1,1) = `(1/√3, 1/√3, 1/√3)`.
- $\cos\theta = (1,0,0) \cdot (1,1,1)/\sqrt{3} = 1/\sqrt{3}$.
- $\theta = \cos^{-1}(1/\sqrt{3}) = 54.74°$.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Bulk-micromachining significance:**
- The 54.74° sidewall produces **predictable V-grooves and pyramidal pits** on (100) wafers.
- Used for **fiber-alignment V-grooves**, **inkjet nozzle pits**, **pyramidal solar cell texturing**, and **diaphragm sidewall definition** in piezoresistive pressure sensors.
- The 54.74° is **set by crystallography** — it is **not a process parameter** and is therefore extremely reproducible batch-to-batch.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanical properties of silicon** relevant to MEMS. Compute the directional Young's modulus along ⟨100⟩, ⟨110⟩, and ⟨111⟩ using the compliance constants `S₁₁ = 7.68 × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹`, `S₁₂ = −2.14 × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹`, `S₄₄ = 12.6 × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **piezoresistive effect in silicon** with its physical origin. Why is silicon preferred over metals for integrated strain gauges in MEMS pressure sensors and accelerometers? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (3 marks) — General discussion:**
Silicon exhibits near-ideal **Hookean elastic behavior** with negligible plastic deformation at room temperature (because **dislocation glide is suppressed** below ~540 °C). Key mechanical properties:

| Property | Value | Significance |
|----------|-------|--------------|
| Density | 2.329 g/cm³ | Low mass → high resonant frequency |
| Young's modulus (avg) | ~165 GPa | High stiffness |
| Yield strength | ~7 GPa | High elastic limit |
| Poisson's ratio | 0.28 | Standard cubic |
| Fracture toughness `K_IC` | 0.7–0.9 MPa·√m | Brittle, no plastic zone |

**Step 2 (4 marks) — Computation:**

- **⟨100⟩:** `1/E = S₁₁ = 7.68 × 10⁻¹²` → `E = 130.2 GPa`
- **⟨110⟩:** `1/E = 0.5 S₁₁ + 0.5 S₁₂ + 0.25 S₄₄ = 5.92 × 10⁻¹²` → `E = 168.9 GPa`
- **⟨111⟩:** `1/E = S₁₁ + 2 S₁₂ + S₄₄/3 = 7.71 × 10⁻¹²` → `E = 187.5 GPa`

(Show all arithmetic steps as in Section 3.4 above.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Physical origin:**
The **piezoresistive effect** is the change in electrical resistivity of a semiconductor when subjected to mechanical stress. In silicon it arises from the **anisotropic shift in the conduction-band valley structure** under strain: applied stress distorts the six equivalent conduction-band minima along ⟨100⟩, lifting degeneracy and shifting carrier populations between valleys, which changes the effective mobility anisotropically. The effect is described by the **π-coefficient tensor**:

$$\frac{\Delta\rho_i}{\rho} = \sum_j \pi_{ij}\,\sigma_j$$

**Step 2 (2.5 marks) — Magnitudes:**
For lightly doped p-type Si along ⟨110⟩: longitudinal `π_l ≈ 71.8 × 10⁻¹¹ Pa⁻¹`; transverse `π_t ≈ −66.3 × 10⁻¹¹ Pa⁻¹`. The **gauge factor**:
$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E \approx 1 + 0.56 + 120 = 121$$

**Step 3 (2.5 marks) — Why silicon is preferred over metals:**

| Criterion | Metal (Constantan) | Doped Silicon |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------|
| Gauge factor | ~2 | **~100–200** |
| Sensitivity | Low | **~50× higher** |
| Hysteresis | None (in range) | None |
| CMOS integration | Impossible | **Monolithic** |
| Size | Macro only | **µm-scale** |
| Temperature drift | Lower | Higher (compensation needed) |

The **~50× higher gauge factor** of silicon enables the detection of sub-nm deflections in micron-scale diaphragms, making silicon indispensable for **integrated MEMS pressure sensors, accelerometers, and AFM cantilevers**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls (Part B — 14-mark questions):**
> 1. **Omitting the units** in the final answer of numerical problems — at least **1 mark** is deducted.
> 2. **Skipping the cubic compliance derivation** for directional Young's modulus — students often quote values without proof and lose **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing `S₁₁, S₁₂, S₄₄`** (compliance, Pa⁻¹) with `C₁₁, C₁₂, C₄₄` (stiffness, Pa). Compliance is the **inverse** of stiffness.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the "why"** behind choosing a wafer orientation — examiners expect an explicit MEMS application link (pressure sensor / gyroscope / AFM tip), not just a statement of etch rate.
> 5. **Mixing notation** `(hkl)` vs `[uvw]` vs `{hkl}` vs `⟨uvw⟩` — strictly: parentheses for plane, brackets for direction, braces for family, angle brackets for direction-family.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section — it is your last-minute revision sheet for the KTU ESE.**

- ☐ Silicon is a **Group IV** element with **diamond cubic (DC)** structure; `Z = 14`, `M = 28.0855 g/mol`.
- ☐ Lattice constant **`a = 5.4309 Å`**; bond length **`d_NN = 2.3517 Å`**; bond angle **`109.47°`**.
- ☐ **8 atoms per unit cell** in the DC lattice; APF = **0.34**; atomic density = **5.00 × 10²² /cm³**.
- ☐ Theoretical density **ρ = 2.329 g/cm³**; matches float-zone Si experimentally.
- ☐ **(100) wafer** → 4-fold symmetry → KOH-etched **54.74°** V-grooves; used in **pressure sensors**.
- ☐ **(110) wafer** → 2-fold symmetry → enables **vertical (90°) sidewalls** via DRIE; used in **gyroscopes**.
- ☐ **(111) wafer** → 6-fold symmetry → etches **~1000× slower** in KOH; used as **etch-stop layer**.
- ☐ **54.74°** = `arctan(√2) = cos⁻¹(1/√3)` — a *crystallographic constant*, not a process parameter.
- ☐ **Young's modulus anisotropy:** `E⟨100⟩ = 130 GPa`, `E⟨110⟩ = 169 GPa`, `E⟨111⟩ = 188 GPa`.
- ☐ **Piezoresistive gauge factor** of doped Si = **~100–200** (vs ~2 for metals) — enables integrated strain gauges.
- ☐ **Yield strength ~7 GPa**, **K_IC ~0.8 MPa·√m** → silicon is **elastic-brittle**, ideal for high-Q resonators.
- ☐ **Thermal conductivity = 150 W/m·K**, **CTE = 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K** → low thermal drift, good for thermal actuators.
- ☐ **Native SiO₂** (~1–2 nm) provides natural surface passivation and is CMOS-compatible.
- ☐ **Miller index convention:** `(hkl)` plane, `[uvw]` direction, `{hkl}` family, `⟨uvw⟩` direction-family.
- ☐ **Compliance values to memorize:** `S₁₁ = 7.68`, `S₁₂ = −2.14`, `S₄₄ = 12.6` (all × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹).
- ☐ **Critical Etch Hierarchy (KOH):** (100) & (110) etch **fast**; (111) etches **slow** → forms {111} sidewalls automatically.
- ☐ **Top 3 MEMS applications:** pressure sensors, inertial sensors (accelerometer/gyroscope), microfluidic channels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Materials: Shape Memory Alloy (SMA), Polymers, Silicon Compounds, etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT744 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Other Materials: Shape Memory Alloy (SMA), Polymers, Silicon Compounds, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Advanced MEMS Materials

Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) primarily rely on silicon as the foundational material. However, the **2024 KTU syllabus** explicitly broadens this scope by introducing **alternative and auxiliary materials** that overcome the inherent limitations of bulk silicon, such as brittleness, lack of biocompatibility, limited stroke/displacement, and poor high-temperature tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The emphasis here is on understanding *why* these alternative materials are chosen for specific MEMS applications. Examiners frequently ask comparative questions between silicon and these advanced materials.

The three major classes of "other materials" covered in this module are:

1. **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA)** — for actuation and motion generation
2. **Polymers** — for flexible, biocompatible, and low-cost MEMS
3. **Silicon Compounds** — for insulation, structural layers, and sacrificial materials

---

## 1.2 Shape Memory Alloys (SMA) — Definition

A **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** is a metallic alloy that exhibits the ability to "remember" its original, pre-deformed shape and return to that shape when subjected to an appropriate thermal stimulus (heating) or, in some cases, upon removal of mechanical stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** Shape Memory Alloys are metallic materials that undergo a solid-to-solid, diffusionless phase transformation between a low-temperature phase called **Martensite** and a high-temperature phase called **Austenite (Parent Phase)**, enabling macroscopic shape recovery upon temperature change.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Origami Metal"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a piece of metal wire that you crumple into a tangled ball. Now, you drop the ball into hot water — and it springs back into a perfectly straight paper-clip shape! An SMA behaves exactly like this. The metal has two internal "personalities": a soft, easily bent personality at low temperature (Martensite) and a rigid, "remembers-its-original-shape" personality at high temperature (Austenite). The transformation between these two personalities is what gives it the magical shape-memory property.

The two most common SMAs in MEMS are:
- **Nitinol (Ni-Ti alloy)** — Nickel-Titanium, used in 90% of practical SMA applications
- **Copper-based SMAs** — Cu-Zn-Al and Cu-Al-Ni, cheaper alternatives

---

## 1.3 Polymers in MEMS — Definition

**Polymers** are long-chain organic molecules composed of repeating monomer units. In MEMS, they serve as **structural, functional, and sacrificial materials** offering advantages such as flexibility, biocompatibility, low cost, and ease of fabrication.

Common MEMS polymers include:
- **SU-8** — epoxy-based negative photoresist for high-aspect-ratio structures
- **PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)** — elastomer for microfluidics and soft lithography
- **PMMA (Polymethyl methacrylate)** — thermoplastic for X-ray lithography
- **Polyimide** — high-temperature polymer for flexible electronics

> [!NOTE]
> **Biocompatibility Note:** Polymers like PDMS and PMMA are widely used in bioMEMS (implants, drug delivery, lab-on-chip) because they do not trigger immune responses in the human body.

---

## 1.4 Silicon Compounds — Definition

**Silicon Compounds** are materials derived from or combined with silicon, used in MEMS for insulation, passivation, structural support, and as sacrificial layers. The key compounds are:

- **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)** — excellent electrical insulator; primary sacrificial layer
- **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)** — hard, wear-resistant insulator; membrane material
- **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** — wide bandgap semiconductor for harsh environments
- **Polysilicon** — polycrystalline silicon used as structural and conductive layer
- **Single Crystal Silicon** — the MEMS substrate (covered in earlier Module 1 sub-topics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not just use silicon everywhere?** Pure silicon is brittle, has low fracture toughness (~0.9 MPa·m^½), and limited stroke range. SiC, in contrast, has fracture toughness of ~3-4 MPa·m^½ and operates above 600°C, making it indispensable for high-temperature, high-stress MEMS such as pressure sensors in jet engines and gyroscopes in automotive safety systems.

---

## 1.5 Why These Materials Matter — A Quick Comparison

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Perspective:** Each material addresses a specific limitation of pure silicon. SMAs provide **large actuation forces and stroke** (silicon-based electrostatic actuators can only move ~10% of their gap). Polymers provide **flexibility and biocompatibility** (silicon is rigid and toxic to cells). Silicon compounds provide **electrical isolation, wear resistance, and high-temperature capability** that single-crystal silicon alone cannot offer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Shape Memory Alloys — Phase Transformation Theory

The core science behind SMAs is the **thermoelastic martensitic transformation**. This is a *diffusionless* (atoms do not migrate) *solid-to-solid* phase change.

### 2.1.1 The Two Crystal Phases

| Phase | Temperature | Crystal Structure | Mechanical Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Austenite (Parent Phase)** | High (above $A_f$) | Ordered B2 (CsCl-type for Nitinol) | Hard, strong, "remembers" original shape |
| **Martensite (Product Phase)** | Low (below $M_f$) | Monoclinic (B19′ for Nitinol) | Soft, easily deformed by twinning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Temperatures — The Four-Point Notation:**
> - $M_s$ = Martensite Start temperature (cooling)
> - $M_f$ = Martensite Finish temperature (cooling)
> - $A_s$ = Austenite Start temperature (heating)
> - $A_f$ = Austenite Finish temperature (heating)

The hysteresis loop is always: $A_f > A_s > M_s > M_f$

For **Nitinol**, typical values are:
- $A_f \approx 110°C$ (can be tuned from −50°C to +110°C by composition)
- Hysteresis width $\Delta T = A_f - M_s \approx 30-50°C$

### 2.1.2 The Shape Memory Effect (SME) — Step-by-Step

1. **High Temperature Shape Setting:** The SMA is held in the desired "parent" geometry and heat-treated at ~500°C (Nitinol). It crystallizes into Austenite.
2. **Cooling Below $M_f$:** The alloy transforms to Martensite without changing macroscopic shape.
3. **Deformation in Martensite State:** The material is mechanically deformed (bent, compressed, twisted). The strain is accommodated by *twinning* (reorientation of martensite variants) — no dislocations, no permanent damage.
4. **Heating Above $A_f$:** The alloy transforms back to Austenite. The crystal structure reverts to the parent phase, and the macroscopic shape recovers — the deformation disappears.
5. **Cooling Again:** It returns to Martensite but **retains** the recovered shape (one-way memory).

### 2.1.3 The Two-Way Shape Memory Effect (TWSME)

In **one-way SME**, the material remembers only the *high-temperature* shape. In **TWSME**, after special "training" (thermomechanical cycling), the material remembers *both* the high-temperature AND low-temperature shapes. The temperature cycle itself causes the shape to oscillate between two configurations.

### 2.1.4 Pseudoelasticity (Superelasticity)

At temperatures **above $A_f$**, applying stress can directly induce Martensite from Austenite *without* changing temperature. Upon removing the stress, the Martensite reverts to Austenite, and the strain recovers. This is called **pseudoelasticity** and allows recoverable strains of up to **8-10%** (compared to ~0.1% for ordinary metals like steel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Point:** The examiner loves to ask, *"What is the maximum recoverable strain of Nitinol vs. ordinary steel?"* — Answer: Nitinol can recover ~8% strain elastically, while steel can only recover ~0.1%. This 80x improvement is why SMA microactuators are so attractive.

---

## 2.2 Polymers — Deep Material Analysis

### 2.2.1 SU-8 — The High-Aspect-Ratio Workhorse

**SU-8** is an epoxy-based **negative photoresist** (cross-links where exposed to UV).

**Key Properties:**
- Young's Modulus: $E \approx 2-4\ \text{GPa}$ (flexible for its size)
- Density: $\rho \approx 1.2\ \text{g/cm}^3$
- Glass Transition Temperature: $T_g \approx 200°C$
- Aspect ratios up to **1:50** (e.g., 100 μm tall, 2 μm wide)
- Chemically resistant to most acids and solvents

**MEMS Applications:** Microfluidic channels, optical waveguides, micro-grippers, neural probes.

### 2.2.2 PDMS — The Elastomer

**PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)** is a silicone-based elastomer, most famously used in **soft lithography** and **lab-on-chip** devices.

**Key Properties:**
- Young's Modulus: $E \approx 0.5-3\ \text{MPa}$ (extremely soft!)
- Elongation at break: ~100-200%
- Optical transparency (transparent in visible spectrum)
- Biocompatible (USP Class VI certified)
- Hydrophobic (can be made hydrophilic by plasma treatment)

### 2.2.3 Comparison Table of Common MEMS Polymers

| Polymer | Type | Young's Modulus | Key Property | Primary MEMS Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SU-8** | Negative photoresist | 2-4 GPa | High aspect ratio | Microfluidics, optical MEMS |
| **PDMS** | Elastomer | 0.5-3 MPa | Extreme flexibility | Soft lithography, bioMEMS |
| **PMMA** | Thermoplastic | 3 GPa | Transparent, X-ray sensitive | X-ray lithography, microfluidics |
| **Polyimide** | Thermoplastic | 2-8 GPa | High $T_g$ (~400°C) | Flexible electronics, insulation |

---

## 2.3 Silicon Compounds — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)

- **Form:** Amorphous (vitreous) when thermally grown; can also be deposited (LPCVD, PECVD)
- **Electrical Resistivity:** $\rho > 10^{16}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ (excellent insulator)
- **Dielectric Constant:** $\epsilon_r \approx 3.9$
- **Etch Rate in HF:** ~1-2 μm/min (this is why it's the *sacrificial layer* in surface micromachining)
- **Role in MEMS:**
  1. **Sacrificial layer** in surface micromachining (etched away in HF to release polysilicon structures)
  2. **Electrical insulation** between conductive layers
  3. **Passivation** layer protecting devices from environmental damage
  4. **Thermal insulation** (low thermal conductivity ~1.4 W/m·K)

### 2.3.2 Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)

- **Form:** Amorphous when LPCVD-deposited at low stress
- **Young's Modulus:** $E \approx 250-300\ \text{GPa}$
- **Residual Stress:** Can be tuned from compressive to tensile by deposition parameters — critical for membrane design
- **Etch Rate in HF:** Very low (etched by *hot phosphoric acid* H₃PO₄ at 180°C instead)
- **Role in MEMS:**
  1. **Pressure sensor membranes** (tunable stress allows flat diaphragms)
  2. **Wear-resistant coatings** (hardness ~20 GPa)
  3. **Hermetic encapsulation** layers

### 2.3.3 Silicon Carbide (SiC)

- **Form:** Polytypes include 3C-SiC (cubic), 4H-SiC, 6H-SiC
- **Bandgap:** $E_g \approx 2.4-3.2\ \text{eV}$ (wide bandgap semiconductor)
- **Young's Modulus:** $E \approx 400-450\ \text{GPa}$
- **Operating Temperature:** Up to **600°C** in air
- **Role in MEMS:**
  1. **High-temperature pressure sensors** for aerospace engines
  2. **Harsh environment** chemical sensors
  3. **High-frequency** RF MEMS resonators

### 2.3.4 Polysilicon

- **Form:** Polycrystalline silicon, deposited by LPCVD
- **Resistivity:** Depends on doping; ~$10^{-3}$ to $10\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$
- **Young's Modulus:** $E \approx 160-170\ \text{GPa}$
- **Role in MEMS:**
  1. **Structural material** in surface micromachining (replaces single-crystal Si in many designs)
  2. **Conductive layer** in integrated MEMS

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols, equations, and boundary conditions needed for the university exam on this topic:**

| # | Equation / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta T = A_f - M_s$ | Hysteresis width of SMA transformation | °C or K |
| 2 | $\epsilon_{rec} \approx 8\%$ | Maximum recoverable strain of Nitinol | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$ | Hooke's law (linear elasticity limit ~0.1%) | Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma_f = \sigma_{app} \cdot Y \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture stress (Griffith criterion) | Pa |
| 5 | $R_{\text{etch, SiO}_2}^{\text{HF}} \approx 1\text{-}2\ \mu\text{m/min}$ | Etch rate of SiO₂ in HF | μm/min |
| 6 | $R_{\text{etch, Si}} \gg R_{\text{etch, SiO}_2}$ | Selectivity condition for sacrificial etching | — |
| 7 | $P = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{a^2} \cdot \Delta P$ | Pressure-deflection for square membrane (small deflection) | Pa |
| 8 | $T_g$ (Polymer) | Glass transition temperature; above this, polymer is rubbery | °C |
| 9 | $\rho_{\text{SiO}_2} > 10^{16}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ | Electrical resistivity of SiO₂ (insulation check) | Ω·cm |
| 10 | $\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$ | Loss tangent of polymer (damping) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Equations 4 and 5 are the most commonly tested. Equation 4 (Griffith) justifies *why* SiC is used in high-stress MEMS (high fracture toughness). Equation 5 justifies *why* SiO₂ is the sacrificial layer (selective HF etch).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Material | Real Product / System | Why This Material? |
|---|---|---|
| **Nitinol SMA** | Self-expanding cardiovascular stents | 8% recoverable strain, biocompatible, body-temperature activated |
| **Nitinol SMA** | Mars Rover Sojourner antenna deployment | Compact stowage, deploys when heated by Sun |
| **PDMS** | Lab-on-chip organ-on-chip devices | Gas-permeable, optically transparent, mimics tissue stiffness |
| **SU-8** | Microneedle arrays for painless drug delivery | High aspect ratio needles, biocompatible |
| **SiC** | Combustion chamber pressure sensor (jet engine) | Survives >500°C where silicon melts/fails |
| **Si₃N₄** | Condenser microphone membrane | Tunable low-stress membrane, high acoustic sensitivity |
| **SiO₂** | MEMS accelerometer proof mass release | Selective HF sacrificial etch releases suspended mass |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hysteresis and Clausius-Clapeyron Relation for SMA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a KTU-favorite 7-mark question.** We derive the *stress-dependence* of the transformation temperature using thermodynamic principles.

### Background

The martensitic transformation in SMAs is a first-order phase transition. The driving force (Gibbs free energy difference) determines the transformation temperature. Applied stress shifts this equilibrium.

### Derivation

At equilibrium between Austenite (A) and Martensite (M), the Gibbs free energies are equal:

$$G_A(T_0, \sigma_0) = G_M(T_0, \sigma_0)$$

where $T_0$ is the equilibrium (stress-free) transformation temperature and $\sigma_0 = 0$ for the unstressed case.

When an external stress $\sigma$ is applied, the Gibbs free energy of each phase changes by the mechanical work term $-V \cdot \sigma \cdot \epsilon$ (where $V$ is molar volume, $\epsilon$ is transformation strain).

$$G_A(T, \sigma) = G_A(T, 0) - V \sigma \epsilon_A$$

$$G_M(T, \sigma) = G_M(T, 0) - V \sigma \epsilon_M$$

At the new equilibrium temperature $T$ under stress $\sigma$:

$$G_A(T, 0) - V \sigma \epsilon_A = G_M(T, 0) - V \sigma \epsilon_M$$

Rearranging:

$$G_A(T, 0) - G_M(T, 0) = V \sigma (\epsilon_A - \epsilon_M)$$

The left side represents the chemical driving force $\Delta G_{chem}$. Expanding around $T_0$ using Taylor series (first order):

$$\Delta G_{chem} = \Delta G_{chem}(T_0) + \left(\frac{\partial \Delta G_{chem}}{\partial T}\right)_p (T - T_0)$$

By definition, at $T_0$: $\Delta G_{chem}(T_0) = 0$.

The derivative is the negative of the transformation entropy change:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G_{chem}}{\partial T}\right)_p = -\Delta S_{tr}$$

Therefore:

$$-\Delta S_{tr} (T - T_0) = V \sigma (\epsilon_A - \epsilon_M)$$

Letting $\Delta \epsilon = \epsilon_M - \epsilon_A$ (the transformation strain), and noting that $\Delta S_{tr} = \Delta H_{tr} / T_0$ (at equilibrium):

$$\frac{\Delta H_{tr}}{T_0} (T - T_0) = V \sigma \Delta \epsilon$$

Solving for the stress-shifted temperature:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H_{tr}}{T_0 \cdot V \cdot \Delta \epsilon}$$

This is the **Clausius-Clapeyron relation for SMA phase transformation**.

### Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta S_{tr}}{\Delta \epsilon \cdot v} = -\frac{\Delta H_{tr}}{T_0 \cdot \Delta \epsilon \cdot v}}$$

where:
- $v$ = molar volume
- $\Delta \epsilon$ = transformation strain (~8% for Nitinol)
- $\Delta H_{tr}$ = latent heat of transformation
- $T_0$ = equilibrium temperature

### Physical Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the equation:** The negative sign means *increasing applied stress lowers the temperature at which Austenite transforms to Martensite*. This is the *inverse* of normal melting (where pressure raises melting point for most materials). In practical MEMS actuator design, this means a tensile stress can "trigger" the SMA transformation at a lower ambient temperature — useful for designing low-power microactuators.

### Numerical Example (For KTU Practice)

For **Nitinol**, take:
- $\Delta H_{tr} = 16720\ \text{J/mol}$
- $T_0 = 350\ \text{K}$
- $v = 1.04 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$
- $\Delta \epsilon = 0.08$

Calculate $d\sigma / dT$:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\frac{16720}{350 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 1.04 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$= -\frac{16720}{2.912 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\approx -5.74 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa/K}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{d\sigma}{dT} \approx -57.4\ \text{MPa/K}}$$

**Interpretation:** Every 1°C increase in temperature requires ~57.4 MPa of additional stress to keep the system at the transformation equilibrium. This is the basis for designing SMA microactuators with predictable force-displacement-temperature characteristics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sacrificial Layer Thickness for Released Structure

In surface micromachining, polysilicon is deposited over a patterned SiO₂ sacrificial layer. After HF release, the gap between the released structure and the substrate equals the SiO₂ thickness. We derive the **maximum allowable sacrificial thickness** to prevent stiction.

The released cantilever (length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, gap $d$) sticks to the substrate if the **elastic restoring force** is overcome by **surface adhesion (van der Waals)** during release.

The elastic restoring force per unit area is approximately:

$$F_{elastic} = \frac{E \cdot t^3 \cdot d}{4 L^4}$$

The adhesion force per unit area (Hamaker constant $A_H \approx 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$ for Si-Si):

$$F_{adhesion} = \frac{A_H}{6\pi d^3}$$

Setting them equal at the **critical (minimum) gap**:

$$\frac{E \cdot t_c^3 \cdot d}{4 L^4} = \frac{A_H}{6\pi d^3}$$

Solving for the critical gap $d$ at which stiction occurs:

$$d^4 = \frac{2 A_H L^4}{3\pi E t^3}$$

$$d_{critical} = \left(\frac{2 A_H L^4}{3\pi E t^3}\right)^{1/4}$$

This is why MEMS designers keep sacrificial layers thin (typically 0.5-2 μm) and use **release drying techniques** (critical point drying, freeze drying) to prevent stiction.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What

| Design Requirement | Recommended Material | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Large force + large stroke actuation | **Nitinol SMA** | 8% strain, 200-500 MPa recovery stress |
| Flexible, biocompatible microfluidic channels | **PDMS** | Low modulus, gas-permeable, transparent |
| High-aspect-ratio micro-needles | **SU-8** | 1:50 aspect ratio, biocompatible |
| Sub-micron precision inertial sensor | **Single-crystal Si** | Low mechanical noise, well-controlled etching |
| High-temperature pressure sensor (>300°C) | **SiC** | Survives 600°C, high Young's modulus |
| Electrical insulation between layers | **SiO₂** or **Si₃N₄** | Resistivity > $10^{16}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ |
| Sacrificial layer for surface micromachining | **SiO₂** | Selective HF etch over silicon |
| Stiff membrane for acoustic sensing | **Si₃N₄** (low-stress LPCVD) | Tunable residual stress, high Young's modulus |
| RF MEMS resonator (>1 GHz) | **SiC** or **diamond** | High acoustic velocity, low loss |
| Substrate for CMOS-integrated MEMS | **Single-crystal Si** | CMOS compatibility, mature process |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Material Selection Logic Tree for MEMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: MEMS Design Requirement] --> B{Primary Function?}

```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: SMA Shape Memory Effect — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Step 1: Parent Shape Heat Treatment at 500C] --> A2[Step 2: Cool Below Mf Martensite Forms]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Apply Mechanical Deformation - Twinning]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Deformed Shape Held in Martensite State]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Heat Above Af - Austenite Forms]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Shape Memory Alloy Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define Shape Memory Alloy (SMA). Name the two crystal phases involved in the shape memory effect and state the temperature relationship that drives the transformation.

**Model Answer:**

A **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** is a metallic alloy that can recover its original, pre-deformed shape upon heating above a critical temperature, due to a reversible solid-to-solid phase transformation.

The two crystal phases are:
1. **Austenite (Parent Phase)** — high-temperature, ordered phase that "remembers" the original shape
2. **Martensite (Product Phase)** — low-temperature, softer phase that allows easy deformation

The transformation is driven by heating above $A_f$ (Austenite Finish Temperature), where the material converts from Martensite back to Austenite, recovering the parent shape.

---

### Question 2: PDMS Properties `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** List any four key properties of PDMS that make it suitable for bioMEMS applications.

**Model Answer:**

Four key properties of **PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)** for bioMEMS:

1. **Biocompatibility** — USP Class VI certified, non-toxic to living cells
2. **Low Young's Modulus** — 0.5-3 MPa, mimics the mechanical softness of human tissue
3. **Optical Transparency** — transparent in visible spectrum, allows real-time optical observation of cells
4. **Gas Permeability** — allows oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange, essential for cell culture inside PDMS microchannels

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

### Question A(a): SMA and Materials Comparison [7 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the Shape Memory Effect in Nitinol with a neat block diagram of the thermomechanical cycle. Compare Nitinol with single-crystal silicon as MEMS actuator materials in terms of (i) maximum recoverable strain, (ii) actuation mechanism, and (iii) fabrication complexity.

**Model Answer:**

**Shape Memory Effect in Nitinol — The Thermomechanical Cycle:**

The shape memory effect in Nitinol (Ni-Ti alloy) occurs in the following steps:

1. **Shape Setting:** The alloy is held in the desired parent geometry and heat-treated at 500°C for 30 minutes. This sets the "memory" shape in the Austenite phase.

2. **Cooling to Martensite:** The alloy is cooled below the Martensite Finish temperature ($M_f \approx 90°C$ for typical Nitinol). The crystal structure transforms to monoclinic Martensite without macroscopic shape change.

3. **Mechanical Deformation:** The material is bent, compressed, or twisted. Because Martensite is soft, large strains (up to 8%) are accommodated by *twinning* — reorientation of martensite variants. No dislocations are created, so the deformation is recoverable.

4. **Heating to Austenite:** The alloy is heated above the Austenite Finish temperature ($A_f \approx 110°C$). The crystal structure reverts to the parent B2 cubic Austenite, and the macroscopic shape recovers the original parent geometry.

5. **Cooling Cycle:** On cooling, the material returns to Martensite but **retains** the recovered shape (one-way effect).

**Comparison Table — Nitinol vs Single-Crystal Silicon Actuator:**

| Parameter | Nitinol SMA | Single-Crystal Silicon |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Max Recoverable Strain** | ~**8%** | ~**0.01-0.1%** (electrostatic) |
| **(ii) Actuation Mechanism** | Thermal phase transformation (Martensite ↔ Austenite) | Electrostatic force between capacitive plates |
| **(iii) Fabrication Complexity** | Moderate (sputtering, annealing, micro-machining) | Very mature (deep RIE, wafer bonding, KOH/TMAH etching) |

**Conclusion:** Nitinol provides 80-800 times larger stroke but requires thermal actuation and has more complex fabrication. Silicon is preferred for high-frequency, low-stroke precision applications like accelerometers and gyroscopes.

---

### Question A(b): Silicon Compounds in MEMS [7 Marks]

**(b)** With suitable examples, explain the roles of SiO₂ and Si₃N₄ in MEMS fabrication. Why is SiO₂ chosen as the sacrificial layer and not Si₃N₄?

**Model Answer:**

**Role of SiO₂ in MEMS:**

Silicon dioxide (SiO₂) serves four primary roles in MEMS:

1. **Sacrificial Layer:** The most important role. A patterned SiO₂ layer is deposited on the substrate, and a structural material (polysilicon) is deposited on top. When the device is immersed in Hydrofluoric acid (HF), the SiO₂ dissolves selectively, releasing the suspended structure. The etch rate is ~1-2 μm/min in concentrated HF.

2. **Electrical Insulation:** SiO₂ has electrical resistivity $> 10^{16}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ and dielectric constant ~3.9, making it an excellent insulator between conductive layers.

3. **Passivation Layer:** Protects underlying silicon from environmental contamination and mechanical damage.

4. **Thermal Insulation:** Low thermal conductivity (~1.4 W/m·K) provides thermal isolation in thermal MEMS such as micro-bolometers and thermal flow sensors.

**Role of Si₃N₄ in MEMS:**

Silicon nitride is primarily used as a **structural and protective material**:

1. **Diaphragm Material:** LPCVD Si₃N₄ with controlled residual stress is used as the sensing membrane in capacitive pressure sensors and condenser microphones. The Young's modulus is 250-300 GPa, providing high stiffness per unit thickness.

2. **Wear-Resistant Coating:** Hardness of ~20 GPa and chemical inertness make it suitable for sliding surfaces in RF MEMS switches and micromotors.

3. **Hermetic Encapsulation:** Low permeability to moisture and gases protects encapsulated resonators and IMUs.

**Why SiO₂ is the Sacrificial Layer, NOT Si₃N₄:**

The choice is determined by **chemical etch selectivity**:

- **SiO₂ etch rate in HF (49%):** ~1-2 μm/min — **FAST**
- **Si₃N₄ etch rate in HF (49%):** ~5-10 nm/min — **NEGLIGIBLE** (etched only by hot H₃PO₄ at 180°C)

When the device is dipped in HF, the SiO₂ sacrificial layer dissolves rapidly, while the Si₃N₄ structural layer (if any) is essentially untouched. This **1000:1 selectivity** allows clean release of suspended structures.

If Si₃N₄ were used as the sacrificial layer, hot phosphoric acid at 180°C would be required, which is incompatible with many polymer structures (PDMS, SU-8) and aluminum metallization. Therefore, SiO₂ is the universal sacrificial material in silicon-based surface micromachining.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

### Question B(a): Polymers in MEMS [7 Marks]

**(a)** Compare SU-8, PDMS, and PMMA as polymer materials for MEMS in terms of Young's modulus, fabrication method, and primary application. Which polymer is most suitable for (i) high-aspect-ratio micro-needles and (ii) soft microfluidic cell-culture chips? Justify.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table — SU-8, PDMS, PMMA:**

| Parameter | SU-8 | PDMS | PMMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Young's Modulus** | 2-4 GPa (rigid) | 0.5-3 MPa (very soft) | 3 GPa (rigid) |
| **Fabrication Method** | UV photolithography (negative resist) | Soft lithography (replica molding from SU-8/Si master) | Hot embossing, injection molding, X-ray LIGA |
| **Primary Application** | High-aspect-ratio microstructures, optical MEMS, microneedles | BioMEMS, microfluidics, organ-on-chip, flexible electronics | Lab-on-chip disposables, X-ray deep lithography |

**Application (i): High-Aspect-Ratio Micro-needles**

**SU-8** is the most suitable polymer for microneedles. Justification:
- SU-8 can achieve aspect ratios of **1:50** (e.g., 1 mm tall, 20 μm wide needles) using standard UV photolithography
- It is **biocompatible** (used in FDA-approved medical devices)
- High Young's modulus (2-4 GPa) provides sufficient mechanical rigidity to penetrate skin without bending
- Chemically resistant to body fluids

**Application (ii): Soft Microfluidic Cell-Culture Chips**

**PDMS** is the most suitable polymer for cell-culture microfluidics. Justification:
- Extremely low Young's modulus (**0.5-3 MPa**) matches the mechanical stiffness of human tissue (~0.5-10 kPa for soft tissues, but cell response is sensitive to substrate stiffness in this range), promoting natural cell behavior
- **Gas permeable** — allows oxygen and CO₂ exchange needed for cell survival
- **Optically transparent** — enables real-time microscopy observation
- **Biocompatible** — does not trigger immune responses
- Easy to bond to glass via plasma treatment (oxygen plasma creates -OH groups for irreversible bonding)

---

### Question B(b): SMA Applications and Pseudoelasticity [7 Marks]

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the pseudoelasticity (superelasticity) phenomenon in SMA. Give two practical MEMS applications where Nitinol SMA is preferred over silicon.

**Model Answer:**

**Pseudoelasticity (Superelasticity):**

Pseudoelasticity is the ability of an SMA to undergo large, fully recoverable strain at temperatures **above $A_f$** (Austenite Finish Temperature), without the need for heating to recover shape.

**Mechanism:**

1. At a temperature above $A_f$, the material is fully in the **Austenite** phase (stable parent state).
2. When mechanical stress is applied, it reaches a critical value at which **stress-induced martensite** forms. This is a diffusionless transformation where Austenite directly converts to Martensite under stress.
3. The transformation accommodates large strain (~**8%** for Nitinol) by changing the crystal structure rather than creating dislocations.
4. When the stress is removed, the material spontaneously reverts from Martensite back to Austenite (because temperature is still above $A_f$).
5. The macroscopic shape is fully recovered — hence the term "pseudoelasticity" (apparent elasticity) or "superelasticity."

**Key Characteristic:** The stress-strain curve shows a wide **plateau region** at constant stress during loading and unloading, indicating large strain recovery at near-constant force. This is fundamentally different from ordinary Hookean elasticity.

**Two Practical MEMS Applications Where Nitinol SMA is Preferred:**

**Application 1: Cardiovascular Stents**
Self-expanding Nitinol stents are crimped into a small diameter at room temperature (Martensite state), inserted into the blocked artery via a catheter, and released. On warming to body temperature (37°C, above $A_f$), the stent transforms to Austenite and self-expands to the original larger diameter, opening the artery. Silicon cannot be used because it cannot undergo such large recoverable strain, and silicon is not biocompatible for long-term implants.

**Application 2: Micro-Actuator for Space Deployable Structures (e.g., Mars Rover Antennas)**
Nitinol SMA wires are coiled compactly for stowage during launch. On reaching Mars, an electric current heats the wire above $A_f$, and it straightens to deploy the antenna or solar panel to its original shape. The advantage is **enormous stowage ratio** (compact storage) and **lightweight**, critical for space payloads. Silicon-based actuators cannot achieve comparable stroke.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Austenite and Martensite phases:** Many students interchange the two. *Always remember:* Austenite = high temperature = rigid = original shape. Martensite = low temperature = soft = deformed shape.
>
> 2. **Confusing pseudoelasticity with shape memory effect:** Shape memory effect requires *heating* for shape recovery; pseudoelasticity requires only *stress removal* (no temperature change).
>
> 3. **Wrong sacrificial layer material:** Students often write "Si₃N₄ is the sacrificial layer" — this is **incorrect**. Si₃N₄ is a structural material. The sacrificial layer is **SiO₂** because of HF selectivity.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to specify units** in numerical derivations (e.g., $d\sigma/dT$ must have units of Pa/K or MPa/K).
>
> 5. **Writing "Nitinol is a polymer"** — Nitinol is a **metallic alloy** (Nickel-Titanium). SMAs are metals, not polymers.
>
> 6. **Missing the bi-directionality aspect** in two-way shape memory effect questions.
>
> 7. **Confusing thermal and electrical conductivities** when discussing MEMS heat sinking.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam:**

### Shape Memory Alloys (SMA)
- ⭐ **Definition:** SMAs remember and recover their original shape upon heating via Martensite ↔ Austenite phase transformation.
- ⭐ **Key Alloy:** Nitinol (Ni-Ti), with 8% maximum recoverable strain.
- ⭐ **Phases:** Austenite (high-T, parent, rigid) ↔ Martensite (low-T, soft, easily deformed).
- ⭐ **Four Critical Temperatures:** $M_s, M_f$ (cooling); $A_s, A_f$ (heating).
- ⭐ **Hysteresis ordering:** $A_f > A_s > M_s > M_f$.
- ⭐ **Two phenomena:** (1) Shape Memory Effect (SME) — heat-triggered; (2) Pseudoelasticity — stress-triggered above $A_f$.
- ⭐ **Two-way memory:** Requires special "training" cycles.
- ⭐ **Applications:** Stents, micro-actuators, space deployable structures.

### Polymers
- ⭐ **SU-8:** Negative photoresist, 1:50 aspect ratio, $E = 2\text{-}4\ \text{GPa}$, $T_g \approx 200°C$, used for microneedles.
- ⭐ **PDMS:** Elastomer, $E = 0.5\text{-}3\ \text{MPa}$, gas-permeable, transparent, biocompatible, used for cell-culture microfluidics.
- ⭐ **PMMA:** Thermoplastic, transparent, used in X-ray LIGA and disposable lab-on-chip.
- ⭐ **Polyimide:** High $T_g \approx 400°C$, used in flexible electronics and harsh environments.
- ⭐ **Glass transition temperature $T_g$:** Above $T_g$, polymer becomes rubbery/soft; below it, polymer is glassy/hard.

### Silicon Compounds
- ⭐ **SiO₂:** Amorphous insulator, $\rho > 10^{16}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$, etch rate in HF ~1-2 μm/min. Roles: sacrificial layer, insulation, passivation, thermal insulation.
- ⭐ **Si₃N₄:** Amorphous structural material, $E = 250\text{-}300\ \text{GPa}$, tunable residual stress. Roles: pressure sensor membrane, wear coating, hermetic encapsulation. Etched by hot H₃PO₄ at 180°C, NOT by HF.
- ⭐ **SiC:** Wide bandgap semiconductor, $E_g = 2.4\text{-}3.2\ \text{eV}$, $E = 400\text{-}450\ \text{GPa}$, operates up to 600°C. Used in high-temperature pressure sensors and harsh environment MEMS.
- ⭐ **Polysilicon:** Polycrystalline Si, $E = 160\text{-}170\ \text{GPa}$, structural + conductive layer in surface micromachining.
- ⭐ **Sacrificial Layer Rule:** SiO₂ is the sacrificial layer because HF selectively etches SiO₂ but not polysilicon or Si₃N₄ (high selectivity > 1000:1).

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- ⭐ **Clausius-Clapeyron Relation:** $d\sigma/dT = -\Delta H_{tr} / (T_0 \cdot \Delta \epsilon \cdot v)$ — derived in Section 3, frequently asked.
- ⭐ **Griffith Fracture Criterion:** $\sigma_f = \sigma_{app} \cdot Y \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$ — explains why SiC is preferred for high-stress MEMS.
- ⭐ **Why these materials?** Each addresses a silicon limitation: SMAs (stroke), polymers (flexibility/biocompatibility), SiC (high temperature), Si₃N₄ (wear resistance), SiO₂ (sacrificial release).

### Frequently Asked KTU Questions (Memory Triggers)
- 🔔 *"Maximum recoverable strain of Nitinol?"* → **8%**
- 🔔 *"Etch rate of SiO₂ in HF?"* → **1-2 μm/min**
- 🔔 *"Young's modulus of PDMS?"* → **0.5-3 MPa**
- 🔔 *"Glass transition temperature of SU-8?"* → **~200°C**
- 🔔 *"Operating temperature of SiC MEMS?"* → **> 600°C**
- 🔔 *"Why SiO₂ is sacrificial, not Si₃N₄?"* → **HF selectivity**
- 🔔 *"Austenite phase crystal structure?"* → **B2 ordered (for Nitinol)**
- 🔔 *"Two-way shape memory requires?"* → **Training/thermomechanical cycling**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial MEMS Structures and Systems: Overview of Commonly Used Mechanical Structures in MEMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT744 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Commercial MEMS Structures and Systems: Overview of Commonly Used Mechanical Structures in MEMS # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Commercial MEMS Structures** are standardized, mass-producible micromechanical building blocks that form the functional core of nearly every commercial Micro-Electro-Mechanical System. These structures are fabricated using silicon (single-crystal, polycrystalline, or amorphous) and related MEMS-compatible materials (polysilicon, silicon nitride, metals, SU-8, Parylene-C) through processes such as surface micromachining, bulk micromachining, and LIGA. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition:** Commonly Used Mechanical Structures in MEMS refer to the foundational, geometric primitives—**cantilevers, fixed-fixed beams (bridges), diaphragms/membranes, comb-finger actuators, microhinges, and plate/spring structures**—that are batch-fabricated using IC-compatible processes and serve as the mechanical transducer elements that convert physical signals (force, pressure, acceleration, displacement, mass) into electrical signals (resistance, capacitance, piezoelectric charge) and vice versa. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microscopic Toolbox" Think of MEMS structures as a **LEGO kit shrunk down a thousand times**: - A **cantilever** is like a diving board sticking out of a swimming pool — fixed on one side, free to bend on the other. It bends when you push the free end down, just like the AFM probe in atomic force microscopes. - A **fixed-fixed beam** is like a plank resting on two stools — both ends clamped, so when force pushes in the middle, it bows upward like an arch. - A **diaphragm** is like a drumhead — clamped around the rim, vibrating when pressure hits it. This is the heart of every pressure sensor and microphone. - A **comb drive** is like two combs held side by side with teeth interleaving — when you slide one comb, the electric field between the teeth changes, producing motion (or sensing displacement). - A **microhinge** is like a microscopic door hinge — letting rigid plates fold out of the plane of the wafer, enabling 3-D structures. > [!NOTE] > **Why these structures dominate commerce:** They are the *only* geometries that can be reliably released from a sacrificial layer (e.g., SiO₂ etched by HF vapor) without sticking (stiction failure), and they can be modeled by well-established Euler-Bernoulli beam theory and Kirchhoff plate theory — making them both manufacturable and mathematically tractable. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Used For | |---|---|---|---| | Young's Modulus of Silicon <110> | $E_{Si}$ | **≈ 130 GPa** (along <110>) and **≈ 170 GPa** (along <100>) | Beam stiffness | | Density of Silicon | $\rho_{Si}$ | **≈ 2330 kg/m³** | Resonant frequency, inertial mass | | Poisson's Ratio of Silicon | $\nu_{Si}$ | **≈ 0.28** | Plate and diaphragm analysis | | Residual Stress | $\sigma_0$ | **10–100 MPa** (typical polysilicon) | Stress-induced curling, beam buckling | | Air Viscosity (at 1 atm) | $\mu_{air}$ | **≈ 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** | Squeeze-film damping | | Quality Factor (vacuum) | $Q$ | **10²–10⁵** | Resonator performance | | Standard Wafer Thickness | $t_{wafer}$ | **525 µm (4-inch), 675 µm (6-inch)** | Bulk micromachining | | Typical Structural Layer Thickness | $t$ | **1–25 µm** | Surface micromachining | > [!TIP] > **For KTU 2024 Numerical Answers:** Always use $E = 130 \text{ GPa}$ for silicon <110> and $\rho = 2330 \text{ kg/m}^3$ unless the question specifies otherwise. Examiners expect these textbook constants. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Commercial MEMS Mechanical Structures ### 2.1.1 Cantilever Beam (Clamped-Free) - **Geometry:** One end rigidly anchored; the other end free. - **Use cases:** AFM probes, chemical/biosensors (resonance shift upon mass loading), RF switches, micro-relays. - **Static deflection** under end load $F$ at the free end (Euler-Bernoulli small-angle assumption): $$ \delta_{cantilever} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} $$ - **Spring constant (stiffness):** $$ k_{cantilever} = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}} $$ - **Resonant frequency (first mode):** $$ f_{0,\text{cant}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \frac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}}} = \frac{0.162\, t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} $$ ### 2.1.2 Fixed-Fixed Beam (Clamped-Clamped / Bridge) - **Geometry:** Both ends anchored. Often suspended over an etched cavity. - **Use cases:** Resonators, filters, accelerometers, pressure sensor diaphragms (when short). - **Deflection** under center load $F$: $$ \delta_{ffb} = \frac{F L^{3}}{192 E I} $$ - **Spring constant:** $$ k_{ffb} = \frac{16 E w t^{3}}{L^{3}} $$ - **Resonant frequency (first mode, considering residual tensile stress $\sigma_0$):** $$ f_{0,ffb} = \frac{1.055\, t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}\,\sqrt{1+\frac{\sigma_{0} L^{2}}{3.4\, E t^{2}}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the residual stress term matters:** In KTU problems, if the question specifies a tensile residual stress, you **must** include the correction factor $\sqrt{1 + \sigma_0 L^2/(3.4 E t^2)}$. Omitting this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. ### 2.1.3 Diaphragm / Membrane (Clamped Plate) - **Geometry:** Square or circular thin plate, edges fully clamped. - **Use cases:** Pressure sensors, microphones, microvalves, micropumps. - **Center deflection** of a square diaphragm (side $a$, thickness $t$, pressure $P$): $$ \delta_{sq} = \frac{0.0151\, P\, a^{4}}{E\, t^{3}}\,(1-\nu^{2})^{-1} $$ - **Center deflection of a circular diaphragm** (radius $R$): $$ \delta_{circ} = \frac{3 P R^{4}(1-\nu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}} $$ - **Pull-in voltage** (for electrostatic diaphragm actuators): $$ V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{0.83\, d_{0}^{3}}{\varepsilon_{0} A}\sqrt{\frac{E t^{3}}{(1-\nu^{2}) d_{0}^{3}}}} $$ ### 2.1.4 Comb-Drive Actuator - **Geometry:** Two sets of interdigitated, laterally moving fingers separated by gap $g$. - **Use cases:** Linear actuators, tunable capacitors, gyroscopes, optical MEMS. - **Lateral capacitance** between $N$ finger pairs, finger overlap length $L_f$: $$ C = N \cdot \frac{2 \varepsilon_{0} t L_{f}}{g} $$ - **Lateral force** at voltage $V$: $$ F_{comb} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} V^{2} = \frac{N \varepsilon_{0} t\, V^{2}}{g} $$ - **Spring constant balance** for stable operation: $F_{comb} < k_{sus} \cdot x_{max}$ ### 2.1.5 Microhinges and Compliant Mechanisms - **Geometry:** Thin flexures (typically < 10 µm) that allow large-angle rotation (often 90°) of rigid plates. - **Use cases:** Out-of-plane mirrors, scanning mirrors, 3-D assembly. - **Stiffness** of a slender hinge of length $L_h$, width $w_h$, thickness $t_h$: $$ k_{hinge} = \frac{E w_h t_h^{3}}{12 L_h} $$ ### 2.1.6 Sealed Cavity / Membrane Stack - **Use cases:** Pressure sensors (absolute reference vacuum cavity), gyroscopes (Coriolis-driven mass), RF switches. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is a board-exam gold mine.** Every KTU question on this module maps to one or more rows below. | Structure | Key Geometric Parameter | Spring Constant $k$ | Resonant Frequency $f_0$ | Stress Correction | |---|---|---|---|---| | Cantilever | Length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$ | $\dfrac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$ | $\dfrac{0.162\, t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | Buckling load $F_{cr}=\dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{4L^{2}}$ | | Fixed-Fixed Beam | $L, w, t$ | $\dfrac{16 E w t^{3}}{L^{3}}$ | $\dfrac{1.055\, t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}\,\sqrt{1+\gamma}$ | $\gamma=\dfrac{\sigma_0 L^2}{3.4 E t^2}$ | | Square Diaphragm | Side $a$, thickness $t$ | $\dfrac{E t^{3}}{0.0151\, a^{4}(1-\nu^{2})}$ | $\dfrac{0.469\, t}{a^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho(1-\nu^{2})}}$ | Biaxial residual stress term | | Circular Diaphragm | Radius $R$, thickness $t$ | $\dfrac{16 E t^{3}}{3 R^{4}(1-\nu^{2})}$ | $\dfrac{0.469\, t}{R^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho(1-\nu^{2})}}$ | Biaxial residual stress term | | Comb Drive | $N$ fingers, gap $g$, $t$, $L_f$ | N/A (force-generating) | N/A (use suspension $k$) | Side instability at $V > V_{side}$ | | Microhinge | $L_h, w_h, t_h$ | $\dfrac{E w_h t_h^{3}}{12 L_h}$ | $\dfrac{t_h}{2 L_h^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | Torsional stiffness $k_\theta=\dfrac{G w_h t_h^{3}}{3 L_h}$ | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Structure | Industrial Product Example | Market Application | |---|---|---| | Cantilever | AFM probe (Bruker, Olympus) | Nanometrology, semiconductor inspection | | Fixed-Fixed Beam | STMicro LIS3DH accelerometer | Smartphones, wearables, automotive airbag | | Diaphragm | Bosch BMP280 barometer | Altimeters, drones, weather stations | | Comb Drive | Analog Devices ADXRS610 gyroscope | Vehicle stability, gaming controllers | | Microhinge | Texas Instruments DLP® mirror array | Projectors, lithography, AR headsets | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Connect:** When asked to "discuss commercial applications," always cite **at least one product name and its manufacturer**. Examiners reward industry-specific awareness. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Spring Constant of a Cantilever Beam **Goal:** Derive $k_{cantilever} = \dfrac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$ from Euler-Bernoulli beam theory. ### Step 1 — Governing Equation For a slender beam under small deflection, the Euler-Bernoulli equation relates bending moment $M(x)$ to curvature: $$ M(x) = E I \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} $$ where $I$ is the second moment of area about the neutral axis. ### Step 2 — Bending Moment from End Load A point load $F$ applied at the free end ($x = L$) produces a bending moment at position $x$: $$ M(x) = F\,(L - x) $$ This is the moment of $F$ about the cross-section at $x$, computed as $F \times$ (lever arm). ### Step 3 — Integrate the Curvature Twice $$ E I \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = F(L - x) $$ Integrate once to get the slope: $$ E I \frac{dy}{dx} = F\left(L x - \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right) + C_1 $$ Boundary Condition 1: Slope is zero at the fixed end $x = 0$: $y'(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$. Integrate a second time to get deflection: $$ E I\, y(x) = F\left(\frac{L x^{2}}{2} - \frac{x^{3}}{6}\right) + C_2 $$ Boundary Condition 2: Deflection is zero at $x = 0$: $y(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$. ### Step 4 — Evaluate at the Free End Set $x = L$: $$ E I\, y(L) = F\left(\frac{L^{3}}{2} - \frac{L^{3}}{6}\right) = F \cdot \frac{L^{3}}{3} $$ $$ \delta = y(L) = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute $I$ for a Rectangular Cross-Section For a beam of width $w$ and thickness $t$ (bending about the thickness axis): $$ I = \frac{w t^{3}}{12} $$ Therefore: $$ \delta = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E \cdot (w t^{3}/12)} = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E w t^{3}} $$ ### Step 6 — Solve for Spring Constant $$ k = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}} \quad \blacksquare $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever Beam **Goal:** Derive $f_{0,cant} = \dfrac{0.162\, t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ from the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation with free-vibration boundary conditions. ### Step 1 — Free Vibration Equation For a uniform beam vibrating at angular frequency $\omega$, with no external load: $$ E I \frac{\partial^{4} y(x,t)}{\partial x^{4}} + \rho A \frac{\partial^{2} y(x,t)}{\partial t^{2}} = 0 $$ ### Step 2 — Separation of Variables Assume $y(x,t) = \phi(x) \cos(\omega t)$. Substituting: $$ \frac{d^{4}\phi}{dx^{4}} = \beta^{4} \phi, \quad \text{where} \quad \beta^{4} = \frac{\rho A \omega^{2}}{E I} $$ ### Step 3 — General Solution The general solution is: $$ \phi(x) = A_{1}\cos(\beta x) + A_{2}\sin(\beta x) + A_{3}\cosh(\beta x) + A_{4}\sinh(\beta x) $$ ### Step 4 — Apply Cantilever Boundary Conditions At the fixed end ($x = 0$): $\phi(0) = 0$ and $\phi'(0) = 0$. At the free end ($x = L$): $\phi''(L) = 0$ (zero bending moment) and $\phi'''(L) = 0$ (zero shear force). Substituting: $$ A_1 + A_3 = 0, \quad A_2 \beta + A_4 \beta = 0 $$ After applying the free-end conditions, the transcendental eigenvalue equation is: $$ \cos(\beta L)\,\cosh(\beta L) = -1 $$ ### Step 5 — Smallest Positive Root The smallest positive solution of the transcendental equation is: $$ \beta_1 L \approx 1.875 $$ Therefore: $$ \beta_1 = \frac{1.875}{L} $$ ### Step 6 — Solve for Frequency $$ \omega_1^{2} = \frac{E I \beta_1^{4}}{\rho A} = \frac{E I}{\rho A} \cdot \frac{(1.875)^{4}}{L^{4}} $$ Compute $(1.875)^4 \approx 12.36$. Substitute $I = wt^3/12$, $A = wt$: $$ \omega_1^{2} = \frac{E (w t^{3}/12)}{(w t) \rho} \cdot \frac{12.36}{L^{4}} = \frac{E t^{2}}{\rho}\cdot\frac{12.36}{12 L^{4}} $$ $$ \omega_1^{2} = \frac{1.030\, E t^{2}}{\rho L^{4}} $$ ### Step 7 — Convert to Hz $$ f_0 = \frac{\omega_1}{2\pi} = \frac{1.015\,t}{2\pi L^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \approx \frac{0.162\, t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \quad \blacksquare $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!NOTE] > **How to use this matrix:** When a KTU question asks "which structure is most suitable for X?", identify the relevant row and read across the columns. | Property | Cantilever | Fixed-Fixed Beam | Diaphragm | Comb Drive | Microhinge | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Stiffness Range | **Low** | **Very High** | **Medium** | **Force-output device** | **Very Low** | | Sensitivity to Mass | **Highest** | Medium | Low | N/A | N/A | | Out-of-plane Deflection | Large | Small | Large | Lateral | Rotational | | Pull-in Risk | High (toward substrate) | Low | Medium (for actuation) | Sideways only | N/A | | Fabrication Difficulty | **Easiest** | Medium | Medium | Hard (alignment) | Hard (assembly) | | Typical Product | AFM tip | Resonator | Pressure sensor | Gyroscope | DLP mirror | | Failure Mode | Stiction | Buckling | Rupture | Side collapse | Hinge fracture | ## 3.4 Damping Considerations (Squeeze-Film Damping) In ambient air, MEMS structures experience **squeeze-film damping** when two closely spaced surfaces move toward each other. The damping coefficient for a plate of area $A$, gap $d_0$, viscosity $\mu$: $$ c = \frac{0.42\,\mu A^{2}}{d_{0}^{3}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Implication for KTU problems:** Always check whether the question asks for vacuum operation or ambient. In vacuum, $c \to 0$, $Q \to \infty$, and the structure behaves as a pure spring-mass system. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MEMS Structural Building Blocks ```mermaid flowchart TB A["MEMS Mechanical Structures"]:::root --> B["Linear Structures"] A --> C["Plate Structures"] A --> D["Rotational Structures"] A --> E["Actuator Structures"] B --> B1["Cantilever Beam
