Examiners give partial marks for labeled layers even if the drawing is rough. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]` **Q:** List any **three commonly used mechanical structures in MEMS** and give one commercial application of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Structure | Commercial Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Cantilever beam** (clamped-free) | Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) probe tip — Bruker Dimension Icon | | 2 | **Diaphragm** (square or circular) | Bosch BMP280 barometric pressure sensor — drones and smartphones | | 3 | **Comb drive** (interdigitated fingers) | Analog Devices ADXRS610 gyroscope — automotive stability control | > **Marking scheme:** 1 mark per correctly listed structure with matching application. **Total: 3 marks.** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional-II]` **Q:** Why is silicon the most widely used material for commercial MEMS structures? Mention any two mechanical properties. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Silicon is the dominant MEMS material because of: 1. **High Young's Modulus (≈ 130–170 GPa)** → mechanically stiff and predictable. 2. **Near-zero mechanical hysteresis / no plastic deformation** → excellent fatigue life (billions of cycles). 3. **IC-fabrication compatibility** → can be batch-processed alongside CMOS, making it economically viable. 4. **Tunable residual stress** through polysilicon deposition parameters. > **Marking scheme:** 2 marks for the explanation + 1 mark for two properties. **Total: 3 marks.** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module-1 Full Question]` **Q:** With neat sketches, explain the working of **(a)** a cantilever beam structure and **(b)** a fixed-fixed beam structure used in commercial MEMS. For each, derive the expression for **(i) spring constant** and **(ii) fundamental resonant frequency**. Compare their suitability for accelerometer and AFM applications. **(7 + 7 marks)** #### Part (a) — Cantilever Beam (7 marks) **Sketch Description (1.5 marks):** - A beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$ is anchored at one end (the fixed/clamped end) and free at the other end. The free end is typically where the deflection is sensed or the mass is loaded. - Coordinate origin at the fixed end, $x$-axis along the beam, $y$-axis perpendicular (deflection direction). **Working Principle (1 mark):** When a force $F$ is applied at the free end (e.g., by an AFM tip-sample interaction, or a mass change), the beam bends downward. The deflection $\delta$ is linearly proportional to $F$ in the small-deflection regime (Euler-Bernoulli regime). This deflection is detected by piezoresistive, piezoelectric, or capacitive methods. **Spring Constant Derivation (2.5 marks):** Start from the moment-curvature equation $M = EI \cdot y''$, apply boundary conditions $y(0) = y'(0) = 0$ at the clamped end. After integrating twice and substituting $x = L$: $$ \delta = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E w t^{3}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_{cantilever} = \frac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}} $$ **Resonant Frequency Derivation (2 marks):** Solve the free-vibration equation $EI \cdot y'''' + \rho A \cdot \ddot{y} = 0$ with cantilever boundary conditions. The eigenvalue equation $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$ has smallest root $\beta_1 L \approx 1.875$, yielding: $$ f_{0,cant} = \frac{0.162\, t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} $$ --- #### Part (b) — Fixed-Fixed Beam (7 marks) **Sketch Description (1.5 marks):** Both ends of the beam are anchored to the substrate. The beam is suspended over a cavity (released after sacrificial etch). It can bend in the middle under transverse loading. **Working Principle (1 mark):** The fixed-fixed beam is much stiffer than a cantilever of the same dimensions. The two anchorages provide restoring force at both ends, so the center deflects much less under the same load. Used in high-Q resonators and as the suspension flexure in accelerometers. **Spring Constant Derivation (2.5 marks):** With boundary conditions $y(0) = y'(0) = y(L) = y'(L) = 0$, applying a center load $F$ at $x = L/2$: $$ \delta_{ffb} = \frac{F L^{3}}{192 E I} $$ Substituting $I = wt^3/12$: $$ k_{ffb} = \frac{192 E I}{L^{3}} = \frac{16 E w t^{3}}{L^{3}} $$ **Resonant Frequency Derivation (2 marks):** Including residual tensile stress $\sigma_0$: $$ f_{0,ffb} = \frac{1.055\, t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}\,\sqrt{1+\frac{\sigma_{0} L^{2}}{3.4\, E t^{2}}} $$ **Comparison for Applications (Included in part marks):** - **Accelerometer:** Fixed-fixed beam is preferred because the **suspension must be stiff enough to limit displacement** under shock and have predictable thermal behavior. The high $k$ ensures linear operation below pull-in. - **AFM Probe:** Cantilever is preferred because the **low spring constant (typically 0.01–1 N/m)** allows sub-nanonewton tip-sample forces. The free end also provides access for the tip. > **Marking scheme allocation:** Sketch (1.5) + Working (1) + Derivation (2.5 + 2) per part. Total = 7 marks × 2 = **14 marks.** --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q:** **(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **comb-drive actuator** with a neat schematic. Derive the expression for the lateral electrostatic force generated. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the construction of a **square diaphragm** used in MEMS pressure sensors. Derive the expression for center deflection under uniform pressure. State any two commercial products using this structure. **(7 marks)** #### Part (a) — Comb-Drive Actuator (7 marks) **Construction (2 marks):** A comb-drive consists of two sets of interlocking, comb-shaped fingers: - **Stator (fixed) fingers** anchored to the substrate. - **Rotor (movable) fingers** attached to a shuttle mass suspended by folded flexures. - Fingers are separated by a small gap $g$ (typically 2–5 µm) and have thickness $t$, overlap length $L_f$, and width $w_f$. - The number of finger pairs is $N$ (typically 20–200). **Working Principle (1.5 marks):** When a voltage $V$ is applied between the stator and rotor fingers, the capacitance of the structure changes with lateral displacement. The energy stored is $U = \frac{1}{2}C V^2$. The lateral force is the negative gradient: $$ F_{x} = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} V^{2} \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} $$ **Derivation of Force (3 marks):** Each finger pair contributes two parallel-plate capacitors (one on each side of the moving finger). The capacitance is: $$ C_{\text{pair}} = \frac{2 \varepsilon_{0} t L_{f}}{g} $$ Differentiating with respect to lateral displacement $x$: $$ \frac{\partial C_{\text{pair}}}{\partial x} = \frac{2 \varepsilon_{0} t}{g} $$ Therefore, for $N$ finger pairs, the total lateral force is: $$ F_{comb} = \frac{1}{2} V^{2} \cdot N \cdot \frac{2 \varepsilon_{0} t}{g} = \frac{N \varepsilon_{0} t\, V^{2}}{g} $$ Note that the force is **independent of $x$** (the overlap length) within the stable travel range — this is the unique advantage of the comb drive. **Schematic Description (0.5 mark):** A diagram showing the stator comb anchored to the substrate, the rotor comb suspended by four folded-beam flexures, electrical contacts at the rotor and stator, with arrows indicating lateral motion under applied voltage. --- #### Part (b) — Square Diaphragm (7 marks) **Construction (2 marks):** A square diaphragm in a MEMS pressure sensor is fabricated by: - **Bulk micromachining** of a silicon wafer from the back side using KOH or DRIE to form a thin membrane (typically 20–50 µm thick) of side $a$. - The diaphragm edges are clamped to the thick silicon rim surrounding the cavity. - A reference vacuum cavity is sealed on one side (absolute pressure sensor) or the diaphragm is exposed to ambient on both sides (gauge pressure sensor). - Piezoresistors are implanted at the high-stress regions near the diaphragm edges. **Working Principle (1 mark):** External pressure $P$ applied to the diaphragm causes it to deflect at the center. This deflection induces bending stress in the diaphragm, which is detected as a change in the piezoresistor resistance. The piezoresistors are arranged in a Wheatstone bridge for differential readout. **Center Deflection Derivation (3 marks):** For a square diaphragm with side $a$, thickness $t$, clamped edges, under uniform pressure $P$, the governing equation is the Kirchhoff plate equation: $$ D\,\nabla^{4} w(x,y) = P $$ where $D = \dfrac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ is the flexural rigidity. For a uniformly loaded square plate with clamped edges, the classical solution (Timoshenko) gives: $$ \delta_{sq} = \frac{0.0151\, P\, a^{4}}{E\, t^{3}}\,(1-\nu^{2})^{-1} $$ For silicon ($\nu = 0.28$), the factor $(1-\nu^2)^{-1} \approx 1.085$, so: $$ \delta_{sq} \approx \frac{0.0164\, P\, a^{4}}{E\, t^{3}} $$ **Commercial Products (1 mark):** 1. **Bosch BMP280** — barometric pressure sensor for smartphones and drones. 2. **Honeywell HSC series** — industrial pressure transducers. 3. **Infineon DPS310** — wearables and IoT. > **Marking scheme allocation:** Construction (2) + Working (1) + Derivation (3) + Products (1) = **7 marks.** --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing fixed-fixed beam with cantilever formulas.** This is the #1 error. Always re-derive from the boundary conditions if you are unsure; do not blindly substitute. > 2. **Forgetting the $(1-\nu^2)$ factor** in diaphragm and plate equations. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this omission. > 3. **Incorrect units in the final numerical answer.** Always check that your $E$ is in Pa, lengths in m, and force in N. A 14-mark question can lose 2 marks for an incorrect unit alone. > 4. **Not specifying the vacuum vs. ambient condition** when calculating $Q$ or resonant frequency. Always mention damping explicitly. > 5. **Skipping the sketch description** in long-answer questions. Even a brief textual description of the geometry earns 1.5 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist - [ ] **Six foundational MEMS structures:** Cantilever, Fixed-Fixed Beam, Diaphragm, Comb Drive, Microhinge, Sealed Membrane. - [ ] **Euler-Bernoulli beam theory** applies for thin beams ($t/L < 0.1$). For thick beams, use Timoshenko beam theory or shear-deformable models. - [ ] **Spring constant formulas** to memorize: - Cantilever: $k = \dfrac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$ - Fixed-Fixed Beam: $k = \dfrac{16 E w t^{3}}{L^{3}}$ (factor of **64×** stiffer than cantilever of same dimensions) - [ ] **Resonant frequency** always scales as $t/L^2 \sqrt{E/\rho}$. Doubling $t$ doubles $f_0$; doubling $L$ quarters $f_0$. - [ ] **Diaphragm deflection** scales as $a^4/t^3$ — a 10% increase in side length gives **46% more deflection**. - [ ] **Comb drive force** is independent of displacement within the stable range, unlike parallel-plate actuators. - [ ] **Residual stress correction** must be applied to fixed-fixed beam frequency when $\sigma_0 \neq 0$. - [ ] **Quality factor $Q$** in vacuum can exceed $10^5$; in air, squeeze-film damping typically limits $Q$ to 10–100. - [ ] **Standard silicon constants for KTU:** $E = 130$ GPa (along $\langle 110 \rangle$), $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³, $\nu = 0.28$. - [ ] **Commercial examples to cite:** Bosch BMP280 (diaphragm), ADXRS610 (comb-drive gyroscope), ST LIS3DH (fixed-fixed beam accelerometer), Bruker AFM (cantilever), TI DLP (microhinge). - [ ] **Stiction failure** is the dominant yield loss in surface-micromachined MEMS — both capillary (release) and electrostatic (operation) varieties. - [ ] **Pull-in voltage** is reached when displacement = $d_0/3$ for parallel-plate, or when electrostatic force overcomes mechanical restoring force. ### 🎯 Common KTU 2024 Numericals to Practice | Problem Type | Key Knowns | Key Unknown | |---|---|---| | Cantilever $k$ & $f_0$ | $L, w, t, E, \rho$ | $k$ and $f_0$ | | Fixed-fixed beam with stress | $L, w, t, E, \rho, \sigma_0$ | $f_0$ with correction | | Diaphragm center deflection | $a, t, P, E, \nu$ | $\delta_{max}$ | | Comb-drive lateral force | $N, g, t, V, \varepsilon_0$ | $F_{comb}$ | | Sensitivity comparison | Two structures, dimensions | Which is stiffer / more sensitive | > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **CO1 (Apply fundamental concepts)** and **CO2 (Design analysis)** for this module. Make sure you can not only recall formulas but also **interpret** which structure is appropriate for a given application. A 2-mark conceptual sub-question often accompanies 12 marks of derivation.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Beams in MEMS

**Formal Definition:** A *beam* in MEMS is a slender structural element whose length is much greater than its cross-sectional dimensions, used as a primary mechanical transducer that converts an input force, mass, or acceleration into a measurable deflection, stress, or change in resonant frequency. Beams are typically **fixed at one or both ends** to a rigid substrate and are fabricated from materials such as **single-crystal silicon, polysilicon, silicon nitride, or metals** like gold and aluminum.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In MEMS, beams are the fundamental building blocks of many sensors and actuators. Their predictable mechanical response makes them ideal for **accelerometers, gyroscopes, RF switches, and chemical/biological sensors**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **ruler held firmly against a table** so that part of it overhangs the edge. If you press down on the overhanging end, the ruler bends. The amount it bends depends on the material, its length, thickness, and width. Now shrink that ruler to a few micrometers in size, make it out of silicon, and you have a **MEMS cantilever beam**! Engineers measure how much this tiny ruler bends or how fast it vibrates to detect forces as small as the weight of a single bacterium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics:**
> - Young's Modulus of Silicon (Si): **E_Si = 169 GPa (⟨100⟩ direction)**
> - Young's Modulus of Polysilicon: **E_poly = 160 GPa**
> - Density of Silicon: **ρ_Si = 2330 kg/m³**
> - Typical beam lengths: **10 µm to 500 µm**
> - Typical beam thicknesses: **0.5 µm to 50 µm**

---

## 1.2 Cantilevers in MEMS

**Formal Definition:** A *cantilever beam* is a beam rigidly fixed (clamped) at **one end only**, leaving the other end free to deflect, vibrate, or interact with the environment. The clamping condition is mathematically modeled as a **fixed boundary** where both deflection and slope are zero.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a **diving board at a swimming pool**. One end is rigidly bolted to the deck (the clamp), and the other end is free to bounce up and down. The diving board is essentially a macroscopic cantilever. A MEMS cantilever works identically — it's a tiny diving board that can sense forces, detect chemicals, or probe surfaces at the nanoscale (this is the working principle of **Atomic Force Microscopy — AFM**).

---

## 1.3 Plates in MEMS

**Formal Definition:** A *plate* is a two-dimensional structural element whose **length and width are of comparable magnitude**, but whose thickness is significantly smaller. Plates in MEMS are typically analyzed using **Kirchhoff-Love plate theory** or **Mindlin plate theory** when the thickness becomes comparable to other dimensions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **drumhead**. It is stretched across a circular frame and can vibrate when struck. A MEMS plate behaves similarly: when a pressure, force, or acceleration is applied, the entire plate surface deforms, and we measure either the static deflection or the dynamic (vibratory) response. Plates are more rigid than beams of equivalent thickness, making them suitable for **pressure sensors, microphones, and microspeakers**.

---

## 1.4 Diaphragms in MEMS

**Formal Definition:** A *diaphragm* is a thin, plate-like structure, usually **circular or rectangular**, that is clamped along its entire perimeter to a rigid support. The pressure difference across the diaphragm causes it to bend or bulge, and this deformation is the basis of most **MEMS pressure sensors**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Consider a **soap bubble membrane** stretched across a ring. If you blow gently into the ring, the membrane bulges outward. A MEMS diaphragm does the same thing when a pressure is applied, except the deflection is on the order of **micrometers**, and we measure it using **piezoresistive, capacitive, or optical techniques**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:**
> - **Beam** → 1D slender element (length ≫ width, thickness)
> - **Plate** → 2D element (length, width ≫ thickness)
> - **Diaphragm** → A plate that is **clamped on all edges** and primarily used to sense **pressure**
> - **Cantilever** → A beam clamped at **only one end**

---

## 1.5 Typical Applications Overview

| Structure | Primary Sensing Function | Typical MEMS Devices |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Cantilever | Force, mass, chemical binding | AFM probes, biosensors, mass sensors |
| Fixed-Fixed Beam | Differential pressure, acceleration | Accelerometers, RF filters |
| Plate | Pressure, acoustic waves | Microphones, speakers, micropumps |
| Diaphragm | Absolute/gauge pressure | Barometric sensors, blood pressure sensors |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Beam Theory Fundamentals

The mechanics of MEMS beams are governed by the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory**, which assumes:
1. Plane cross-sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after deformation.
2. Shear deformation is negligible (valid when length ≫ thickness).
3. The beam material is **linear elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law: σ = Eε).

The governing differential equation for static deflection of a uniform beam under distributed load q(x) is:

$$EI \frac{d^4w(x)}{dx^4} = q(x)$$

Where:
- **E** = Young's Modulus (Pa)
- **I** = Area moment of inertia (m⁴)
- **w(x)** = transverse deflection (m)
- **q(x)** = distributed load per unit length (N/m)

For a rectangular cross-section of width *b* and thickness *h*:

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{12}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the moment of inertia scales with the **cube of thickness (h³)**. This is why MEMS designers can dramatically alter beam stiffness by changing the thickness by just a few micrometers!

---

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions

| Boundary Type | Deflection (w) | Slope (dw/dx) | Bending Moment (M) | Shear Force (V) |
|---------------|----------------|---------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Clamped (Fixed)** | w = 0 | dw/dx = 0 | M ≠ 0 | V ≠ 0 |
| **Free** | w ≠ 0 | dw/dx ≠ 0 | M = 0 | V = 0 |
| **Simply Supported** | w = 0 | dw/dx ≠ 0 | M = 0 | V ≠ 0 |

---

## 2.3 Cantilever Beam Analysis

For a **cantilever beam of length L** subjected to a **point load F at the free end**:

**Static Tip Deflection:**

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{FL^3}{3EI}$$

**Tip Stiffness (Spring Constant):**

$$k = \frac{3EI}{L^3} = \frac{Ebh^3}{4L^3}$$

**Resonant Frequency (First Mode):**

$$f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$$

Where **1.875** is the first root of the transcendental equation cos(βL)·cosh(βL) = -1, and **ρA** is the mass per unit length.

---

## 2.4 Fixed-Fixed (Clamped-Clamped) Beam Analysis

For a beam clamped at **both ends**, the first-mode resonant frequency uses a higher eigenvalue (**4.73**):

$$f_1 = \frac{4.73^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$$

**Effective Spring Constant:**

$$k_{eff} \approx \frac{16EI}{L^3} = \frac{4Ebh^3}{L^3}$$

---

## 2.5 Circular Diaphragm Analysis

For a **circular diaphragm of radius a and thickness h** under uniform pressure **P**, the central deflection is given by:

$$w_{max} = \frac{Pa^4}{64D}$$

Where the **flexural rigidity D** of the plate is:

$$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

And **ν** is Poisson's ratio (for silicon, **ν ≈ 0.28**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of D:** The flexural rigidity is a measure of the plate's resistance to bending. The factor **(1-ν²)** accounts for the constraint offered by the surrounding plate material in two-dimensional deformation.

---

## 2.6 Rectangular Diaphragm Analysis

For a **rectangular diaphragm of dimensions a × b** under uniform pressure P, the center deflection is:

$$w_{max} = \alpha \frac{Pa^4}{Eh^3}$$

Where **α** is a dimensionless coefficient that depends on the **aspect ratio (a/b)** and **boundary conditions**. For a square plate (a = b) clamped on all edges:

$$w_{max} = \frac{0.00126 \cdot P a^4}{D}$$

---

## 2.7 Stress Analysis in Diaphragms

The **maximum stress** in a circular diaphragm occurs at the clamped edge (at radius = a):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3Pa^2}{4h^2}$$

This is a critical design parameter because exceeding the **yield strength of silicon (~7 GPa)** or the fracture limit will cause catastrophic failure.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $I = \frac{bh^3}{12}$ | Moment of inertia (rectangular beam) | All beam calculations |
| 2 | $\delta = \frac{FL^3}{3EI}$ | Cantilever tip deflection | Static force sensing |
| 3 | $k = \frac{Ebh^3}{4L^3}$ | Cantilever spring constant | Force/displacement sensor |
| 4 | $f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$ | Cantilever resonant frequency | Resonant mass sensor |
| 5 | $f_1 = \frac{4.73^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$ | Fixed-fixed beam resonant frequency | Resonator, filter |
| 6 | $D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Flexural rigidity (plate) | Diaphragm deflection |
| 7 | $w_{max} = \frac{Pa^4}{64D}$ | Circular diaphragm center deflection | Pressure sensor |
| 8 | $\sigma_{max} = \frac{3Pa^2}{4h^2}$ | Maximum stress in circular diaphragm | Failure analysis |
| 9 | $Q = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} \cdot \Delta d$ | Capacitance change (parallel plate) | Capacitive readout |
| 10 | $f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency (mass-spring) | All resonant sensors |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

These structures form the **backbone of modern MEMS**:

1. **Automotive Industry:** Accelerometers (fixed-fixed beams) for airbag deployment and electronic stability control.
2. **Medical Devices:** Pressure sensors (diaphragms) for blood pressure monitoring and intracranial pressure measurement.
3. **Consumer Electronics:** MEMS microphones (diaphragms) in smartphones and hearing aids.
4. **Scientific Instrumentation:** AFM cantilevers for surface imaging at sub-nanometer resolution.
5. **Industrial Process Control:** Mass flow sensors using cantilever thermal principles.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cantilever Beam Tip Deflection Under Point Load

**Problem Setup:** A cantilever beam of length L, rectangular cross-section (width b, thickness h), is rigidly clamped at x = 0 and subjected to a vertical point load F at x = L. Derive the tip deflection.

### Step 1: Apply the Euler-Bernoulli Equation

The governing differential equation for static beam bending is:

$$EI \frac{d^4w}{dx^4} = q(x)$$

**Explanation:** Since the load is a point load at the free end, we use the **equivalent form with bending moment**:

$$EI \frac{d^2w}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

### Step 2: Determine the Bending Moment Distribution

At a distance x from the clamped end, the bending moment caused by the point load F at the tip is:

$$M(x) = -F(L - x)$$

**Explanation:** The moment arm is the distance from the section at x to the load at L, which is (L - x). The negative sign follows the standard sign convention (sagging moment is negative in some conventions; we will use the magnitude for clarity).

### Step 3: Integrate the Moment Equation

Substituting M(x) into the beam equation:

$$EI \frac{d^2w}{dx^2} = -F(L - x)$$

Integrate once to obtain the **slope**:

$$EI \frac{dw}{dx} = -F\left(Lx - \frac{x^2}{2}\right) + C_1$$

**Explanation:** Integration of -F(L - x) = -FL + Fx gives -FLx + Fx²/2 plus a constant of integration C₁.

### Step 4: Apply Boundary Condition 1 (Slope at Clamped End)

At x = 0, the slope is zero (cantilever is rigidly clamped):

$$\left.\frac{dw}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0 \implies C_1 = 0$$

**Explanation:** A clamped end prevents any rotation, hence dw/dx = 0 at x = 0.

### Step 5: Integrate Again to Get Deflection

$$EI \cdot w(x) = -F\left(\frac{Lx^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{6}\right) + C_2$$

**Explanation:** Integrating the slope expression term-by-term: ∫(-FLx) dx = -FLx²/2, and ∫(Fx²/2) dx = Fx³/6.

### Step 6: Apply Boundary Condition 2 (Deflection at Clamped End)

At x = 0, the deflection is zero:

$$w(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$$

### Step 7: Compute Tip Deflection at x = L

$$w(L) = \frac{-F}{EI}\left(\frac{L \cdot L^2}{2} - \frac{L^3}{6}\right) = \frac{-F}{EI}\left(\frac{L^3}{2} - \frac{L^3}{6}\right)$$

$$w(L) = \frac{-F}{EI}\left(\frac{3L^3 - L^3}{6}\right) = \frac{-F}{EI} \cdot \frac{2L^3}{6} = \frac{-FL^3}{3EI}$$

Taking the magnitude:

$$\boxed{\delta_{tip} = \frac{FL^3}{3EI}}$$

**Final Result:** The tip deflection of a cantilever under an end load F is proportional to L³ and inversely proportional to EI.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever Beam

**Problem Setup:** Find the natural frequency of a uniform cantilever beam of length L, mass per unit length μ = ρA.

### Step 1: Governing Dynamic Equation

For free vibration, the beam equation is:

$$EI \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

### Step 2: Assume Harmonic Solution

Assume a separable solution:

$$w(x,t) = W(x) \cdot e^{j\omega t}$$

Substituting:

$$EI \frac{d^4 W(x)}{dx^4} - \rho A \omega^2 W(x) = 0$$

### Step 3: Define the Wave Number β

Let:

$$\beta^4 = \frac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI} \implies \omega = \beta^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

### Step 4: General Solution Form

The general solution is:

$$W(x) = A\cos(\beta x) + B\sin(\beta x) + C\cosh(\beta x) + D\sinh(\beta x)$$

### Step 5: Apply Boundary Conditions

For a cantilever clamped at x = 0 and free at x = L:

| Location | Condition | Equation |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| x = 0 | Deflection = 0 | W(0) = 0 |
| x = 0 | Slope = 0 | W'(0) = 0 |
| x = L | Moment = 0 | W''(L) = 0 |
| x = L | Shear = 0 | W'''(L) = 0 |

### Step 6: Solve the Eigenvalue Equation

From the four boundary conditions, we arrive at the characteristic equation:

$$\cos(\beta L) \cosh(\beta L) = -1$$

### Step 7: First Root of the Characteristic Equation

The **smallest positive root** of this transcendental equation is:

$$\beta_1 L = 1.875$$

### Step 8: Final Expression for First Resonant Frequency

$$f_1 = \frac{\omega_1}{2\pi} = \frac{\beta_1^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

$$\boxed{f_1 = \frac{(1.875)^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Center Deflection of a Clamped Circular Diaphragm

**Problem Setup:** A circular diaphragm of radius a, thickness h, is clamped at its edge and subjected to a uniform pressure P. Derive the central deflection w(0).

### Step 1: Governing Plate Equation (Axisymmetric)

In polar coordinates, for an axisymmetric load:

$$D \left( \frac{d^2w}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{dw}{dr} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dw}{dr}\right) = P$$

Equivalently, the **biharmonic equation** becomes:

$$D \nabla^4 w = P$$

Where:

$$\nabla^4 = \left(\frac{d^2}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\right)^2$$

### Step 2: Assume Polynomial Trial Solution

For a uniformly loaded circular plate, we assume:

$$w(r) = c_0 + c_1 r^2 + c_2 r^4$$

**Explanation:** A 4th-order polynomial is needed to satisfy the biharmonic equation.

### Step 3: Apply the Governing Equation

Computing the Laplacian of w(r):

$$\nabla^2 w = 4c_2 r^2 + 4c_1 + \frac{1}{r}(2c_1 r + 4c_2 r^3) = 16c_2 r^2 + 8c_1$$

Applying $\nabla^2$ again:

$$\nabla^4 w = 64 c_2$$

### Step 4: Equate to Pressure Loading

$$64 D c_2 = P \implies c_2 = \frac{P}{64D}$$

### Step 5: Apply Boundary Conditions

| Condition | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------|
| w(a) = 0 | Deflection zero at edge | Clamped edge |
| w'(a) = 0 | Slope zero at edge | Clamped edge (no rotation) |

From the first condition:

$$c_0 + c_1 a^2 + c_2 a^4 = 0$$

From the second condition (dw/dr = 2c₁r + 4c₂r³ at r = a):

$$2c_1 a + 4c_2 a^3 = 0 \implies c_1 = -2c_2 a^2$$

### Step 6: Solve for Constants

Substituting c₁ into the first condition:

$$c_0 - 2c_2 a^4 + c_2 a^4 = 0 \implies c_0 = c_2 a^4$$

So:

$$c_0 = \frac{Pa^4}{64D}, \quad c_1 = -\frac{Pa^2}{32D}, \quad c_2 = \frac{P}{64D}$$

### Step 7: Central Deflection at r = 0

$$w(0) = c_0 = \frac{Pa^4}{64D}$$

Substituting $D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$:

$$\boxed{w_{max} = \frac{3Pa^4(1-\nu^2)}{16Eh^3}}$$

**Explanation:** The central deflection scales as the **4th power of the diaphragm radius** and **inversely as the cube of thickness**. This is the most important design relation in MEMS pressure sensor design!

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison of MEMS Mechanical Structures

| Parameter | Cantilever | Fixed-Fixed Beam | Circular Diaphragm | Rectangular Diaphragm |
|-----------|------------|------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Deflection Formula** | $\frac{FL^3}{3EI}$ | $\frac{FL^3}{192EI}$ (center) | $\frac{3Pa^4(1-\nu^2)}{16Eh^3}$ | $\frac{\alpha Pa^4}{Eh^3}$ |
| **Spring Constant (k)** | $\frac{3EI}{L^3}$ | $\frac{16EI}{L^3}$ | $\frac{16\pi D}{a^2}$ | $\frac{C \cdot Eh^3}{a^2}$ |
| **Eigenvalue (β₁L or 1st root)** | 1.875 | 4.730 | 10.22 | ≈ 36 (for a=b) |
| **Max Stress Location** | Clamped end (top fiber) | Both clamped ends | Clamped edge | Mid-edges |
| **Typical Application** | AFM, biosensor | Accelerometer, RF filter | Pressure sensor | Microphone, micropump |
| **Sensitivity to Length** | High (∝ L³) | High (∝ L³) | High (∝ a⁴) | High (∝ a⁴) |
| **Sensitivity to Thickness** | High (∝ 1/h³) | High (∝ 1/h³) | Very High (∝ 1/h³) | Very High (∝ 1/h³) |
