## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / PEMRT744 / Q1]**

**Q1.** Define protective coating in MEMS. List any three materials commonly used as protective coatings.

**Model Answer:**
> A **protective coating** in MEMS is a thin-film barrier deposited over a fabricated micro-structure to shield it from environmental contaminants such as moisture, ions, particulates, and biological species, while still allowing the physical signal of interest to reach the sensing element.
>
> Three common coating materials are:
> 1. **Parylene C** — conformal polymer, deposited by CVD, used for moisture barrier and bio-compatibility.
> 2. **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)** — inorganic ceramic deposited by PECVD/LPCVD, excellent barrier against moisture and ions.
> 3. **SU-8** — photo-patternable epoxy used as both structural and protective layer.

---

**Q2.** What is media isolation? Why is it essential in industrial pressure sensors?

**Model Answer:**
> **Media isolation** is the technique of interposing a chemically inert barrier (typically a metal diaphragm with an oil-filled chamber) between the process fluid and the MEMS sensor, so that only pressure is transmitted hydraulically.
>
> It is essential because:
> - The MEMS die cannot tolerate direct contact with **corrosive, abrasive, or hot process media**.
> - It protects the sensor from **over-pressure**, **thermal shock**, and **chemical attack**.
> - It allows **field replacement** of the sensor without breaking the process line.
> - It enables use of standard MEMS dies across vastly different media (oil, water, acids, gases).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / PEMRT744]*

**(a)** Explain the need for protective coating in MEMS devices. Discuss **Parylene C** deposition in detail, including its properties, deposition method, and at least three MEMS applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat schematic, describe the **oil-filled media isolation** technique used in industrial pressure sensors. Derive the expression for the centre deflection of the isolation diaphragm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Need for Protective Coating:**
MEMS devices contain suspended micro-structures (beams, membranes, cantilevers) and exposed metal interconnects. These are vulnerable to:
- **Moisture ingress** causing stiction and corrosion.
- **Particulate contamination** causing friction and wear.
- **Bio-fouling** in biomedical implants.
- **Tribological wear** during repeated actuation.
- **Ionic migration** causing dielectric breakdown.

A protective coating provides a thin, conformal, defect-free barrier that mitigates these failure modes without significantly affecting device performance.

**Parylene C — Properties:**
- **Chemical Name:** Poly-monochloro-para-xylylene.
- **Density:** ~1.29 g/cm³.
- **Young's Modulus:** ~3.2 GPa.
- **Tensile Strength:** ~70 MPa.
- **Elongation at Break:** ~200 % (highly flexible).
- **Dielectric Strength:** ~220 V/µm.
- **Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR):** ~0.5 g/m²/day (excellent moisture barrier).
- **Bio-compatible** (USP Class VI certified, FDA approved).

**Deposition Method — Gorham Process (CVD):**
1. **Sublimation:** Solid dimer (di-para-xylylene) is vaporized at ~150 °C.
2. **Pyrolysis:** The vapour is heated to ~680 °C, cleaving the dimer into reactive monomers.
3. **Deposition:** Monomers condense on the cold substrate (~25 °C), spontaneously polymerizing into a uniform, pin-hole-free film.
4. **Thickness control** is achieved by varying the initial dimer mass.

**Three MEMS Applications:**
1. **Implantable Bio-MEMS** — neural probes, intra-cardiac pressure sensors.
2. **Inertial Sensors** — accelerometer and gyroscope die protection.
3. **Microfluidic Devices** — channel wall coating to prevent adsorption.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Schematic of Oil-Filled Media Isolation (refer Section 4.2 for Mermaid schematic).**

The pressure sensor consists of:
1. A **welded stainless-steel process port** at the inlet.
2. An **isolation diaphragm** (typically SS316L or Hastelloy C) sealing the process side.
3. A chamber filled with **silicone oil** (inert, non-conductive).
4. A **silicon MEMS die** with piezoresistive bridges forming a Wheatstone bridge.
5. An **ASIC** for amplification and temperature compensation.
6. A **hermetic header** with electrical feedthroughs.

**Working:** Process pressure $p$ acts on the outer diaphragm. The diaphragm deflects, pressurizing the silicone oil. The oil transmits this pressure to the silicon die, where the piezoresistors change resistance, producing an output voltage proportional to $p$.

**Derivation — Centre Deflection of Isolation Diaphragm:**

For a clamped circular plate of radius $a$, thickness $h$, under uniform pressure $p$:

$$
D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}
$$

$$
D \nabla^4 w(r) = p
$$

With boundary conditions $w(a) = 0$ and $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\Big|_{r=a} = 0$, the solution is:

$$
w(r) = \frac{p}{64 D}(a^2 - r^2)^2
$$

Maximum at centre ($r = 0$):

$$
w_{max} = \frac{p a^4}{64 D}
$$

Substituting $D$:

$$
w_{max} = \frac{3 p a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E h^3} \approx \frac{0.662\, p\, a^4}{E h^3}
$$

**Stiffness of the diaphragm:**

$$
k_{iso} = \frac{16 \pi D}{a^2}
$$

This stiffness, in series with the silicon die stiffness, determines the overall sensitivity of the sensor.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare **hermetic** and **non-hermetic** packaging of MEMS devices. Discuss at least four bonding techniques used in hermetic packaging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various types of **media isolation techniques** used in MEMS pressure sensors. Discuss the role of silicone oil and the trade-offs involved. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Hermetic vs Non-Hermetic Packaging:**

| Parameter | Hermetic | Non-Hermetic |
|---|---|---|
| Seal integrity | Helium leak < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s | Permeable to moisture over years |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Process complexity | High (wafer-level bonding) | Low (moulding, epoxy) |
| Lifetime | > 20 years | 5 to 10 years |
| Typical use | Medical, aerospace, automotive under-hood | Consumer electronics |
| Materials | Glass, metal, ceramic | Epoxy, polymer |

**Bonding Techniques for Hermetic Packaging:**

1. **Anodic Bonding:** A glass substrate (e.g., Pyrex 7740) is bonded to silicon at ~400 °C with ~1 kV applied across the stack. Na⁺ ions migrate from glass, leaving an oxygen-depleted layer that bonds to silicon. Used in pressure sensor packages.

2. **Glass-Frit Bonding:** Low-melting-point glass paste is screen-printed, then reflowed at ~450 °C to bond two wafers. Tolerates surface non-planarity.

3. **Eutectic Bonding:** Au-Si eutectic at ~363 °C forms a hermetic metal-metal bond. Used in high-reliability RF MEMS.

4. **Metal-to-Metal Thermocompression:** Au-Au or Cu-Cu bonding under high pressure at elevated temperature. Excellent hermeticity, used in advanced MEMS packaging.

5. **Solder Bonding:** Au-Sn, Sn-Ag-Cu solders for low-stress hermetic seals, especially in optical MEMS.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Types of Media Isolation in MEMS Pressure Sensors:**

1. **Welded Metal Diaphragm Isolation** — A thin SS316L or Hastelloy diaphragm is welded to the sensor body. The space between the diaphragm and the silicon die is filled with silicone oil. Most common in industrial transmitters.

2. **Elastomer Isolation** — A Viton or EPDM rubber diaphragm isolates the sensor. Used for low-pressure, mildly corrosive media. Cheaper, but temperature-limited (~150 °C max).

3. **Glass-to-Metal Seal Isolation** — A Kovar-glass header isolates electrical feedthroughs. Used in vacuum and medical sensors.

4. **Inline Chemical Isolation** — A PTFE or PFA diaphragm chemically compatible with strong acids and bases. Used in chemical dosing.

5. **Direct Welded Isolation** — For ultra-high pressure (>1000 bar), the silicon die is housed in a thick-walled metal block with a welded diaphragm and no oil (or minimal oil).

**Role of Silicone Oil:**
- **Hydraulic pressure transmission** from the isolation diaphragm to the silicon die.
- **Thermal expansion compensation** (matched to the silicon die).
- **Damping** of high-frequency pressure spikes.
- **Galvanic isolation** between the process media and the electrically active die.

**Trade-offs:**
| Property | Lower Viscosity Oil | Higher Viscosity Oil |
|---|---|---|
| Dynamic response | Fast | Slow |
| Temperature stability | Worse | Better |
| Bubble risk | Higher | Lower |
| Pressure transmission loss | Lower | Higher |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "protective coating" with "media isolation."** Coating is at the **die (micro) scale**; isolation is at the **package (macro) scale**. Many students swap them and lose 2 to 3 marks.
> 2. **Always state units** in derivations — examiners specifically deduct for missing units in the formula sheet.
> 3. **Centre deflection formula** for a clamped circular plate is $\dfrac{0.662\, p\, a^4}{E h^3}$ — students often write the rectangular plate formula by mistake and lose marks.
> 4. **Do not say "parylene is deposited by spin-coating."** It is a CVD process (Gorham process). This is a very common error.
> 5. In Question A(b), you must draw the **schematic** — leaving it out loses a guaranteed 1 to 2 marks even if your derivation is perfect.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Protective coating** = thin-film barrier at the **die level**.
- ✅ **Media isolation** = package-level barrier (diaphragm + oil fill).
- ✅ **Parylene C** is deposited by **CVD (Gorham process)** — *not* spin coating.
- ✅ **Residual stress formula:** $\sigma_{res} = \dfrac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}(\alpha_s - \alpha_f)\Delta T$.
- ✅ **Frequency shift due to coating:** $\Delta f \approx -f_0 \dfrac{\Delta m}{2m}$ (mass loading effect).
- ✅ **Centre deflection of isolation diaphragm:** $w_{max} = \dfrac{0.662\, p\, a^4}{E h^3}$.
- ✅ **Hermetic leak rate threshold:** **< 1 × 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s** (MIL-STD-883).
- ✅ **Common isolation diaphragm materials:** SS316L, Hastelloy C, Titanium, PTFE, Viton.
- ✅ **Common coating materials:** Parylene C, Si₃N₄, SiO₂, SU-8, Polyimide, ALD Al₂O₃, Au, Pt.
- ✅ **Hermetic bonding methods:** Anodic, glass-frit, eutectic (Au-Si), thermocompression, solder.
- ✅ **Silicone oil function:** Hydraulic transmission + thermal compensation + damping.
- ✅ **Sensitivity scales as $a^4/h^3$** for circular diaphragms — design accordingly.
- ✅ **Stiction** = primary failure mode that protective coatings prevent.
- ✅ **Trade-off rule:** thicker diaphragm → higher media compatibility but lower sensitivity.
- ✅ **IP ratings IP65/IP67/IP68** describe media isolation effectiveness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wafer Level Packaging and Die Level Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT744 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wafer Level Packaging and Die Level Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Packaging in MEMS

**Packaging** in Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) refers to the science and engineering of enclosing, interconnecting, and protecting a micro-scale die (chip) so that it can be reliably interfaced with the macroscopic external world (PCB, board, or system). It is often quoted in industry that **packaging can account for 40% to 70% of the total MEMS product cost**, making it one of the most critical steps in the MEMS value chain.

In the context of **KTU PEMRT744 Module 4**, packaging is classified based on **the level at which the packaging operation is performed** in the semiconductor/MEMS process flow:
1. **Wafer Level Packaging (WLP)** — performed on the entire wafer before dicing.
2. **Die Level Packaging (DLP)** — performed on individual singulated dies after wafer dicing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT744 – Module 4):**
> Students must be able to *compare and contrast wafer-level and die-level packaging techniques, identify suitable applications, describe process flows, and evaluate trade-offs in cost, performance, footprint, and reliability.*

---

## 1.2 Wafer Level Packaging (WLP)

### Formal Definition
**Wafer Level Packaging (WLP)** is the technology of packaging an integrated circuit (IC) or MEMS device while it is still part of the un-singulated wafer, i.e., **before the wafer is diced into individual chips**. The entire packaging operation — including **Redistribution Layer (RDL) routing**, **Under-Bump Metallurgy (UBM)**, **solder ball/bump formation**, and in some cases **encapsulation** — is completed at the wafer scale.

The result is that the final packaged chip is essentially the **same size as the original die**, hence the term **Chip-Scale Package (CSP)**. The package outline does not exceed **1.2× the original die area** as per the **JEDEC JESD22-A114** definition of a true CSP.

### Sub-Categories of WLP
- **Fan-In WLP (FI-WLP):** The I/O pads are redistributed within the original die footprint. Limited I/O density (typically <200 I/O).
- **Fan-Out WLP (FO-WLP):** The wafer is first reconstituted (molded) with epoxy compound to expand the area; I/O pads are then fanned out beyond the die edges, allowing **higher I/O counts (>200)**.
- **Through-Silicon Via (TSV)-based 2.5D/3D WLP:** Vertical interconnects through the silicon for stacking.

### Real-World Analogy
> Imagine baking a large rectangular **sheet cake** (the wafer). In **WLP**, you decorate the entire cake first (icing, writing, design) and then cut it into individual slices — each slice already has the decoration on it. In contrast, **DLP** is like cutting the cake first and then decorating each slice individually.

---

## 1.3 Die Level Packaging (DLP)

### Formal Definition
**Die Level Packaging (DLP)**, also known as **Conventional Packaging** or **Post-Dicing Packaging**, is the traditional method where the wafer is first **diced** into individual chips, and then each chip is separately mounted onto a **lead frame or substrate**, electrically interconnected (via **wire bonding**, **flip-chip**, or **Tape Automated Bonding (TAB)**), and finally encapsulated within a **molding compound** or **ceramic/hermetic package**.

### Common DLP Package Families
- **Dual In-line Package (DIP)** — through-hole, legacy.
- **Small Outline Integrated Circuit (SOIC)**
- **Quad Flat Package (QFP)** / **Low-profile QFP (LQFP)**
- **Quad Flat No-leads (QFN / DFN)**
- **Ball Grid Array (BGA)** — for high I/O counts.
- **Ceramic Dual In-line Package (CERDIP)** — for hermetic MEMS.

### Real-World Analogy
> As mentioned earlier, **DLP is the "cut-then-decorate" approach**. Each slice (die) is handled, mounted, wired, and sealed one at a time — much like a craftsman assembling each piece of furniture individually rather than using a pre-fabricated modular system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exams:**
> - **WLP → Whole wafer is packaged first, then diced.**
> - **DLP → Wafer is diced first, then each die is packaged individually.**
> - WLP packages are essentially **chip-scale**; DLP packages are **larger than the die** due to the substrate and molding area.

---

## 1.4 Why This Distinction Matters in MEMS

MEMS devices are unique because they often need:
- **Hermetic sealing** (to protect movable structures from moisture/dust).
- **Cavities or caps** (to allow mechanical movement without contact).
- **Optical windows** (for MEMS optical sensors, micromirrors).
- **Low-pressure or vacuum environments** (for gyroscopes, resonators).

Both WLP and DLP can address these, but through **different fabrication routes**, leading to different cost structures and reliability profiles.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wafer Level Packaging (WLP) — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Process Flow (Standard Fan-In WLP)

1. **Wafer Preparation** — incoming wafer with fabricated MEMS/IC structures and passivation.
2. **Polymer Passivation / Stress Buffer Layer** — typically **polyimide** or **benzocyclobutene (BCB)**.
3. **RDL (Redistribution Layer) Deposition** — sputtering/electroplating of **Cu** to reroute peripheral pads to an area-array layout.
4. **UBM (Under-Bump Metallurgy)** — adhesion layer (**Ti** or **TiW**) + barrier (**Ni**) + wettable layer (**Cu** or **Au**).
5. **Solder Bump Deposition** — either **screening of solder paste**, **electroplating**, or **ball drop**.
6. **Reflow** — to form spherical solder balls (typical diameter: **200–500 µm**).
7. **Wafer-Level Testing (Wafer Sort / Final Test)** — electrical test before dicing.
8. **Back-grinding & Dicing** — wafer thinned to **<100 µm** and then singulated.
9. **Tape & Reel** — for SMT assembly.

### 2.1.2 Fan-Out WLP (FO-WLP) Process

1. Reconstitution of carrier with separated dies in molding compound.
2. Carrier removal.
3. RDL formation on the molded artificial wafer.
4. Bumping and dicing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Term:** *eWLB* (embedded Wafer Level Ball Grid Array) by Infineon and *FOWLP* by TSMC are the most common commercial implementations.

---

## 2.2 Die Level Packaging (DLP) — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Process Flow (Generic)

1. **Wafer Dicing** — saw or laser dicing into individual dies.
2. **Die Attach** — die bonded onto **lead frame** or **organic substrate** using **conductive epoxy** or **solder**.
3. **Wire Bonding** — fine gold (Au) or copper (Cu) wires (**25 µm diameter**) connect die pads to lead fingers.
4. **Encapsulation** — transfer molding with **epoxy molding compound (EMC)**.
5. **Marking & Solder Plating** — for QFP-style lead finish.
6. **Singulation** — trim and form leads (for leaded packages).
7. **Final Test (FT)** — electrical test after packaging.

### 2.2.2 Why DLP is Still Relevant
- Allows **Known-Good-Die (KGD)** testing before packaging → higher assembly yield.
- Supports a **much wider range of package styles** (hermetic, ceramic, plastic).
- Enables **flexible I/O counts** from 8 to >1000.
- Compatible with **mature supply chain** (OSAT providers like ASE, Amkor, JCET).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates all equations and key numerical parameters relevant to WLP and DLP from the KTU 2024 syllabus perspective.

> **Note on Table Syntax:** To prevent markdown table breakage, all vertical-bar-style mathematical symbols (such as absolute value, modulus, "divides", or magnitude) are written using `\vert` instead of the pipe character.

| # | Parameter / Equation | Formula / Value | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost per packaged die (DLP) | $$C_{DLP} = \dfrac{C_{wafer} + N_d \cdot (C_{attach} + C_{wire} + C_{mold})}{N_d \cdot Y_{DLP}}$$ | $USD$ | Captures assembly + test cost in die-level flow. |
| 2 | Cost per packaged die (WLP) | $$C_{WLP} = \dfrac{C_{wafer} + C_{WLCSP}}{Y_{WLP}}$$ | $USD$ | Single cost event at wafer scale; cheaper per die at high volume. |
| 3 | Compound Yield | $$Y = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$ | dimensionless | Product of individual step yields; critical for cost modeling. |
| 4 | Package-to-Die Area Ratio (CSP Limit) | $$\rho = \dfrac{A_{package}}{A_{die}} \le 1.2$$ | dimensionless | JEDEC definition of a true chip-scale package. |
| 5 | Solder Bump Pitch (WLP) | $$P_{bump} \in [0.4, 0.8] \text{ mm}$$ | mm | Determines I/O density; smaller pitch → more I/O. |
| 6 | Maximum I/O Count (Fan-In) | $$N_{IO,FI} \approx \dfrac{\pi R_{die}^2}{P_{bump}^2}$$ | count | Limits fan-in WLP to roughly 200 I/O. |
| 7 | Maximum I/O Count (Fan-Out) | $$N_{IO,FO} \approx \dfrac{\pi R_{reconstituted}^2}{P_{bump}^2}$$ | count | Fan-out decouples I/O count from die size. |
| 8 | Thermal Resistance (Junction-to-Board) | $$\theta_{JB} = \dfrac{T_J - T_B}{P}$$ | $^\circ C / W$ | Lower is better; WLP typically has lower $\theta_{JB}$ due to short interconnects. |
| 9 | Parasitic Inductance (Wire Bond, DLP) | $$L_{wb} \approx 1 \text{ nH/mm}$$ | nH/mm | Long wires increase inductance; degrades RF performance. |
| 10 | Parasitic Inductance (Solder Bump, WLP) | $$L_{bump} \approx 0.1 \text{ nH}$$ | nH | ~10× lower than wire bonds — preferred for RF MEMS. |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Preferred Technology | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Consumer MEMS (accelerometers in smartphones) | **WLP (Fan-In)** | Smallest footprint, lowest cost at high volume. |
| Smart Watch / Wearable PMICs | **WLP (Fan-Out)** | Higher I/O + small size. |
| Automotive MEMS pressure sensors | **DLP (Ceramic/Metal)** | Hermeticity and harsh-environment reliability. |
| RF MEMS / BAW filters | **WLP with hermetic cap** | Low parasitics, hermetic sealing essential. |
| MEMS Gyroscopes | **DLP (Ceramic LGA)** | Vacuum packaging and KGD guarantee. |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive mathematical and analytical derivations** required to quantitatively compare WLP and DLP — a high-weightage topic in KTU 2024 scheme board exams.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Cost-per-Die Model

### 3.1.1 Statement
The total cost of producing one functional packaged die must include **wafer cost, processing cost, and yield losses**. We derive a generalized model and apply it to both WLP and DLP.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $C_w$ = Cost of one incoming wafer (USD).
- $N_d$ = Number of gross dies on the wafer.
- $Y_w$ = Wafer fabrication yield (fraction).
- $Y_a$ = Assembly / packaging yield (fraction).
- $C_a$ = Cost of assembly per die (USD).
- $C_t$ = Cost of test per die (USD).

**Step 1: Number of good dies per wafer after fabrication.**

$$
N_{good} = N_d \cdot Y_w
$$

**Step 2: Cost contribution per die from the wafer.**

$$
C_{wafer,per\,die} = \dfrac{C_w}{N_d \cdot Y_w}
$$

**Step 3: Cost contribution per die from assembly.**

$$
C_{assembly,per\,die} = \dfrac{C_a}{Y_a}
$$

**Step 4: Total cost per functional packaged die.**

$$
C_{total} = \dfrac{C_w}{N_d \cdot Y_w} + \dfrac{C_a}{Y_a} + C_t
$$

**Step 5: Specialization for WLP.**

Since WLP processes the entire wafer as a single unit, there is **no per-die assembly step** in the conventional sense. The packaging cost $C_{WLP}$ is amortized across all dies on the wafer:

$$
C_{WLP,total} = \dfrac{C_w + C_{pack,wafer}}{N_d \cdot Y_w \cdot Y_{bump}} + C_t
$$

where $Y_{bump}$ is the bumping yield (typically $> 99.5\%$ for mature processes).

**Step 6: Specialization for DLP.**

In DLP, every individual die is processed. The assembly cost per die ($C_a$) is fixed:

$$
C_{DLP,total} = \dfrac{C_w}{N_d \cdot Y_w} + \dfrac{C_a}{Y_a} + C_t
$$

**Step 7: Numerical Comparison Example.**

Assume: $C_w = \$1500$, $N_d = 1000$, $Y_w = 0.9$, $C_{pack,wafer} = \$200$, $Y_{bump} = 0.99$, $C_a = \$0.15$ (DLP per die), $Y_a = 0.99$, $C_t = \$0.05$.

For WLP:

$$
C_{WLP,total} = \dfrac{1500 + 200}{1000 \times 0.9 \times 0.99} + 0.05
$$

$$
C_{WLP,total} = \dfrac{1700}{891} + 0.05 \approx 1.908 + 0.05 = \$1.958
$$

For DLP:

$$
C_{DLP,total} = \dfrac{1500}{1000 \times 0.9} + \dfrac{0.15}{0.99} + 0.05
$$

$$
C_{DLP,total} = 1.667 + 0.152 + 0.05 = \$1.869
$$

**Inference:** At moderate yield, DLP appears marginally cheaper because of the high fixed cost $C_{pack,wafer}$ in WLP. However, as volume scales, $C_{pack,wafer}$ amortizes further, and the **breakeven volume** below which WLP wins can be computed:

**Step 8: Breakeven Volume.**

Setting $C_{WLP,total} = C_{DLP,total}$:

$$
\dfrac{C_w + C_{pack,wafer}}{N_d \cdot Y_w \cdot Y_{bump}} + C_t = \dfrac{C_w}{N_d \cdot Y_w} + \dfrac{C_a}{Y_a} + C_t
$$

Simplifying and solving for the boundary condition yields:

$$
N_d \ge \dfrac{C_{pack,wafer} \cdot Y_a}{C_a \cdot Y_{bump}}
$$

Plugging in the values:

$$
N_d \ge \dfrac{200 \times 0.99}{0.15 \times 0.99} = \dfrac{200}{0.15} \approx 1333 \text{ dies/wafer}
$$

Thus, **WLP becomes more cost-effective than DLP when the wafer contains roughly 1333 or more gross dies** — typically true for advanced nodes with large wafer sizes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum I/O Count in Fan-In WLP

### 3.2.1 Statement
Determine the maximum number of I/O bumps that can be placed inside the die area for a square die of side $L_{die}$ and bump pitch $P$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Effective die radius.**

For area-array bumping, we approximate the die as a circle of radius:

$$
R_{die} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{2}
$$

**Step 2: Number of bump positions in a regular area array.**

$$
N_{IO} = \dfrac{\pi R_{die}^2}{P^2} = \dfrac{\pi L_{die}^2}{4 P^2}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example.**

For $L_{die} = 5 \text{ mm}$ and $P = 0.5 \text{ mm}$:

$$
N_{IO} = \dfrac{\pi \times 25}{4 \times 0.25} = \dfrac{78.54}{1} \approx 78 \text{ I/Os}
$$

This is well within the practical Fan-In limit of ~200 I/O. For higher counts, **Fan-Out WLP** is mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Thermal Resistance Comparison (Analytical)

### 3.3.1 Statement
Compare the junction-to-board thermal resistance of a WLP vs. a QFN (DLP).

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: WLP thermal path.**
Heat flows: Die → Solder bump → PCB.

$$
\theta_{JB,WLP} = \theta_{bump} + \theta_{pad}
$$

**Step 2: DLP (QFN) thermal path.**
Heat flows: Die → Die attach → Lead frame pad → Solder joint → PCB.

$$
\theta_{JB,DLP} = \theta_{die\,attach} + \theta_{lead\,frame} + \theta_{solder\,joint}
$$

**Step 3: Typical values.**

| Layer | $\theta$ ($\,^\circ C / W$) |
|---|---|
| Solder Bump (WLP) | **0.5** |
| Solder Joint (QFN) | **1.0** |
| Die Attach | **2.0** |
| Lead Frame Spread | **1.5** |

**Step 4: Computation.**

$$
\theta_{JB,WLP} \approx 0.5 + 0.2 = 0.7 \, ^\circ C/W
$$

$$
\theta_{JB,DLP} \approx 2.0 + 1.5 + 1.0 = 4.5 \, ^\circ C/W
$$

**Step 5: Inference.**

WLP offers **roughly 6× better thermal performance**, which is critical for power-hungry MEMS like **BAW filters** and **PMIC dies**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (WLP vs. DLP)

| Comparison Axis | Wafer Level Packaging (WLP) | Die Level Packaging (DLP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Location** | Before dicing (on full wafer) | After dicing (per die) |
| **Package Size** | Chip-scale ($\rho \le 1.2$) | Larger than die ($\rho \ge 2$) |
| **I/O Count** | Limited (Fan-In) / High (Fan-Out) | Very flexible (8 to >1000) |
| **Electrical Performance** | Excellent (low parasitics) | Moderate to poor |
| **Thermal Performance** | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Cost at High Volume** | Lower | Higher |
| **Cost at Low Volume** | Higher (setup cost) | Lower (no wafer tooling) |
| **KGD Testing** | Difficult at wafer level | Easy (test before packaging) |
| **Hermetic Sealing** | Possible via wafer bonding | Standard ceramic/glass-metal |
| **Time-to-Market** | Slower (wafer-level tooling) | Faster (mature supply chain) |
| **MEMS-Specific Use** | Accelerometers, gyros (with cap), RF | Pressure sensors, optical MEMS |
| **Typical Suppliers** | TSMC, Amkor, STATS ChipPAC | ASE, Amkor, JCET, SPIL |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides **architectural block-flow diagrams** rendered in Mermaid, depicting the process flows and comparison logic of WLP and DLP.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Semiconductor-to-Packaged-Die Flow

```mermaid
    C1 --> C3[RDL and Bumping]
    C3 --> C4[Wafer Sort Testing]
    C4 --> C5[Back Grinding and Dicing]

    D1 --> D2[Die Attach on Substrate]
    D2 --> D3[Wire Bonding or Flip Chip]
    D3 --> D4[Transfer Molding]
    D4 --> D5[Marking and Singulation]

    D5 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Final Test]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section follows the **exact KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern** for a 14-mark question with internal choice, as specified in the KTU 2024 Scheme regulations.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Wafer Level Packaging (WLP). List any two advantages of WLP over Die Level Packaging.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Wafer Level Packaging (WLP) is the technology of packaging an integrated circuit or MEMS device **while it is still part of the un-singulated wafer**, before wafer dicing. The package outline is essentially the same as the die outline, making it a **Chip-Scale Package (CSP)**.

> **Advantages (1.5 Marks — any two):**
> 1. **Smaller form factor** — package area is $\le 1.2\times$ the die area (true CSP).
> 2. **Lower per-die cost at high volume** — packaging is done once at wafer scale.
> 3. **Better electrical and thermal performance** — short solder-bump interconnects reduce parasitic inductance (~**0.1 nH** vs. ~**1 nH/mm** for wire bonds).
> 4. **Simplified SMT assembly** — the packaged wafer can be directly soldered to the PCB without an additional substrate.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is Die Level Packaging (DLP)? Mention the main steps involved.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Die Level Packaging (DLP) is the conventional packaging technique where the wafer is first **diced** into individual dies, and each die is then individually mounted, interconnected, and encapsulated to form the final package.

> **Main Steps (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Wafer Dicing** — sawing or laser-cutting the wafer into individual dies.
> 2. **Die Attach** — bonding the die onto a **lead frame** or **organic substrate** using conductive epoxy or solder.
> 3. **Interconnection** — via **wire bonding** (Au/Cu) or **flip-chip** soldering.
> 4. **Encapsulation** — transfer molding with epoxy molding compound (EMC) or hermetic sealing in ceramic/metal.
> 5. **Singulation & Final Test** — trimming leads, marking, and electrical testing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: With neat block diagrams, explain the Wafer Level Packaging (WLP) process flow. Discuss Fan-In and Fan-Out WLP techniques and compare their advantages and limitations. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — WLP Process Flow & Fan-In WLP (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

> **Step 1 — WLP Definition (1 Mark):**
> WLP is the packaging of ICs/MEMS at the wafer scale, before dicing, such that the final package outline is essentially the same as the die (chip-scale package, $\rho \le 1.2$).

> **Step 2 — WLP Process Flow (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Wafer Incoming Inspection** — defect/metrology check.
> 2. **Polymer Stress-Buffer Layer** — Polyimide or BCB spin-coating and curing.
> 3. **RDL (Redistribution Layer) Deposition** — sputtering + electroplating of Cu (~5 µm) to reroute peripheral pads to area-array.
> 4. **UBM (Under-Bump Metallurgy)** — Ti/TiW adhesion layer + Ni barrier + Cu wettable layer.
> 5. **Bumping** — solder paste printing or electroplating, followed by reflow to form spherical bumps.
> 6. **Wafer Sort / Final Test** — electrical probing of every die on the wafer.
> 7. **Back-Grinding** — wafer thinning to <100 µm.
> 8. **Dicing & Tape-and-Reel** — singulation and packaging for shipment.

> **Step 3 — Fan-In WLP Explanation (3 Marks):**
> - In Fan-In WLP, the redistribution routing is **confined within the original die area**.
> - The I/O bumps are placed inside the die outline.
> - **Limitation:** Maximum I/O count is bounded by the die area:
>
> $$
> N_{IO,FI} \approx \dfrac{\pi R_{die}^2}{P_{bump}^2}
> $$
>
> For a 5 mm × 5 mm die at 0.5 mm pitch, $N_{IO,FI} \approx 78$ — well below 200.
> - **Advantage:** Cheapest WLP variant; smallest possible footprint.

#### Part (b) — Fan-Out WLP & Comparison (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Fan-Out WLP Explanation (3 Marks):**
> - Fan-Out WLP uses a **reconstituted wafer** — individual known-good dies are placed face-down on a temporary carrier, and the gaps are filled with epoxy molding compound to form an artificial wafer.
> - The carrier is removed; RDL is then formed over the molded surface, **allowing routing beyond the die edges**.
> - **Advantage:** Higher I/O density and decoupling of die size from bump count:
>
> $$
> N_{IO,FO} \approx \dfrac{\pi R_{reconstituted}^2}{P_{bump}^2}
> $$
>
> - **Commercial examples:** Infineon *eWLB*, TSMC *InFO*, Amkor *SLIM* and *SWIFT*.

> **Step 2 — Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Fan-In WLP | Fan-Out WLP |
|---|---|---|
| I/O Density | Low (≤200) | High (>200) |
| Process Complexity | Low | High (reconstitution) |
| Wafer Bow / Stress | Low | Moderate to high |
| Cost per Die | Lowest | Higher |
| Typical Apps | PMIC, audio codecs | Apps processors, baseband |

> **Step 3 — Concluding Statement (1 Mark):**
> Fan-Out WLP is the **preferred next-generation solution** for high-I/O, mixed-technology, and 3D heterogeneous integration in advanced MEMS and IC packaging.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q: Describe Die Level Packaging (DLP) with its process steps. Compare WLP and DLP based on cost, electrical performance, I/O density, and MEMS hermetic packaging capability. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Die Level Packaging Process (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Definition (1 Mark):**
> DLP is the **post-dicing packaging** technique where each singulated die is individually mounted on a substrate/lead frame, interconnected, and encapsulated.

> **Step 2 — DLP Process Steps (4 Marks):**
> 1. **Wafer Dicing** — saw or laser singulation.
> 2. **Die Attach** — conductive epoxy or solder onto lead frame / substrate.
> 3. **Curing** — thermal cure of die-attach material.
> 4. **Wire Bonding** — thermosonic bonding of Au/Cu wires (~25 µm diameter) using capillary tool.
> 5. **Molding** — transfer molding with epoxy molding compound at ~175 °C.
> 6. **Post-Mold Cure** — to relieve stress.
> 7. **Marking & De-flash** — laser marking and removal of mold flash.
> 8. **Singulation (Trim & Form)** — separating individual units and forming leads.
> 9. **Final Test** — full electrical test.

> **Step 3 — Common Package Families (2 Marks):**
> - **Leaded:** DIP, SOIC, QFP, LQFP.
> - **Leadless:** QFN, DFN.
> - **Area-array:** BGA, LGA, PoP.
> - **Hermetic for MEMS:** CERDIP, ceramic LGA, metal-can TO-style.

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Cost Comparison (2 Marks):**
> - **WLP** has higher upfront tooling cost (wafer-level equipment) but **lower per-die cost at high volume**.
> - **DLP** has low setup cost but **higher per-die cost** because each die is processed individually.

> **Step 2 — Electrical Performance (2 Marks):**
> - WLP solder bumps have **$L \approx 0.1$ nH** and **$R \approx 5$ m$\Omega$** → excellent for RF MEMS.
> - DLP wire bonds have **$L \approx 1$ nH/mm** and **$R \approx 30$ m$\Omega$** → lossy at GHz frequencies.

> **Step 3 — I/O Density (1.5 Marks):**
> - Fan-In WLP: limited to ~200 I/O.
> - Fan-Out WLP: >500 I/O possible.
> - DLP BGA: >1000 I/O achievable for large packages.

> **Step 4 — Hermetic MEMS Packaging (1.5 Marks):**
> - **WLP** supports **wafer-to-wafer bonding** (anodic, glass-frit, Au-Sn eutectic, Al-Ge) for MEMS capping — enables batch-fabricated vacuum cavities.
> - **DLP** requires individual ceramic/metal packages for hermeticity — more expensive but allows **Known-Good-Die (KGD)** insertion before sealing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing **Wafer Level Packaging** with **Wafer Scale Integration (WSI)** — they are different. WSI builds the *entire system* on a wafer; WLP just packages the existing die at wafer scale.
> 2. ❌ Stating "WLP is always cheaper than DLP" — **false**. WLP wins only at high volume; for low-volume or mixed-technology devices, DLP is cheaper.
> 3. ❌ Saying "DLP cannot be hermetic" — **false**. Ceramic DLP packages (CERDIP, LGA) are widely used for **automotive and aerospace MEMS**.
> 4. ❌ Forgetting to mention **UBM and RDL** when describing WLP — these are **mandatory** marks in 14-mark questions.
> 5. ❌ Drawing cross-sections but **forgetting to label** them — KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks for unlabeled diagrams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 2-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

### Core Concepts
- **WLP = Wafer-level packaging** (before dicing) → Chip-Scale Package (CSP).
- **DLP = Die-level packaging** (after dicing) → Conventional packages like QFN, BGA.
- WLP package area $\le 1.2 \times$ die area (JEDEC CSP definition).

### Process Essentials
- **WLP key steps:** Passivation → RDL → UBM → Bumping → Test → Back-grind → Dice.
- **DLP key steps:** Dice → Die-attach → Wire-bond → Mold → Singulate → Test.
- **Fan-Out WLP** = reconstituted wafer + molding compound → enables >200 I/O.

### Quantitative Anchors
- Wire-bond inductance ≈ **1 nH/mm**.
- Solder-bump inductance ≈ **0.1 nH**.
- WLP thermal resistance ≈ **0.7 °C/W** vs. DLP QFN ≈ **4.5 °C/W**.
- Cost breakeven: WLP wins when **dies/wafer** $\gtrsim C_{pack,wafer}/(C_a \cdot Y_{bump}/Y_a)$.

### MEMS-Specific Highlights
- **Hermetic WLP** uses **wafer-to-wafer bonding** (anodic, glass-frit, eutectic).
- **Hermetic DLP** uses **ceramic packages** for high-reliability MEMS.
- WLP is dominant in **consumer MEMS** (smartphone sensors); DLP dominates **automotive/aerospace MEMS**.

### KTU Exam Triggers
- If question asks "compare" → make a **table**.
- If question asks "with neat diagram" → **Mermaid block or labeled schematic mandatory**.
- Always mention **UBM + RDL** when discussing WLP.
- Always mention **die-attach + wire-bond + mold** when discussing DLP.
- Always quote a **numerical example** to score full 14 marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT744 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calibration and Compensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Calibration — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the documented process of comparing a MEMS sensor's output (response) against a **traceable reference standard** of known accuracy, under **specified environmental and operational conditions**, to determine, quantify, and document the device's measurement characteristics (sensitivity, offset, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, drift).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, calibration is treated as a **quantitative metrology procedure** that establishes the **input–output transfer function** $y = f(x)$ of the transducer so that any subsequent reading can be confidently converted into an SI-traceable physical quantity.

## 1.2 Compensation — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compensation** is the set of corrective actions (hardware circuitry, software algorithms, or hybrid techniques) applied to a MEMS sensor's raw output to **nullify or minimize errors** arising from parasitic sensitivities, environmental influences (temperature, humidity, pressure), ageing, packaging stress, and process-induced non-idealities.

Compensation does **not** change the physical transducer; it **post-processes** the signal so the final output ideally represents only the desired measurand.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Weighing-Scale Analogy**
> Imagine a slightly inaccurate bathroom scale that always reads **+1.5 kg** heavier than your actual weight and varies by a few hundred grams depending on the room temperature. 
> - **Calibration** = placing certified **standard weights (e.g., 5 kg, 20 kg, 50 kg)** on the scale, recording the readings, and writing down the *correction curve* (offset & gain).
> - **Compensation** = adding a small **thermistor circuit** that slightly trims the reading whenever the bathroom is warm, and storing a **lookup table** in a microcontroller to subtract that 1.5 kg bias.
> 
> In MEMS, calibration gives you the *correction map*; compensation is the *real-time engine* that applies it.

## 1.4 Why Both Are Essential in MEMS

- **Inherent imperfections:** Micromachined structures suffer from residual stress, doping gradients, die-attach stress, and wafer-level variability.
- **Environmental coupling:** MEMS resonant frequencies, piezoresistive coefficients, and capacitive gaps drift with **temperature** (typically a few hundred ppm/°C).
- **Process spread:** Capacitive gaps of ±10 % and piezoresistor values of ±20 % are common across a wafer.
- **Cost-effective mass production:** Calibration + on-chip compensation enable inexpensive, accurate devices without per-unit hand-trimming.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** *Calibration* answers *"How inaccurate is the device?"*; *Compensation* answers *"How do we correct it in real time?"*

## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics (Always Quote in Bold)

- **Sensitivity (S):** output change per unit measurand (V/Pa, V/g, pF/μm).
- **Offset (Zero Drift):** output when the measurand is zero.
- **Non-Linearity (NL):** maximum deviation from the best-fit straight line, expressed as **% Full-Scale Span (FSS)**.
- **Hysteresis (H):** maximum difference between upscale and downscale readings at the same input.
- **Repeatability / Reproducibility:** scatter of independent measurements under identical conditions.
- **Accuracy:** closeness of the mean reading to the true value.
- **Resolution:** smallest detectable change in the measurand.
- **Temperature Coefficients of Offset (TCO) and Span (TCS).**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Calibration Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Define the measurand range** ($x_{\min}$ to $x_{\max}$) and the required accuracy.
2. **Choose reference standards** traceable to national/international laboratories (e.g., NIST, NPL).
3. **Apply at least 5–11 known input points** in both ascending and descending sweeps.
4. **Record raw outputs** $y_{\text{raw},i}$ and the **reference values** $y_{\text{ref},i}$.
5. **Fit a model** (linear, polynomial, or piecewise) using **least-squares regression**.
6. **Compute static metrics:** non-linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, TCO, TCS.
7. **Generate calibration coefficients** (gain $G$, offset $O$, and polynomial coefficients).
8. **Validate** with a second, independent set of measurements.
9. **Document** with traceability certificates and uncertainty budgets.

## 2.2 Static vs Dynamic Calibration

| Aspect | Static Calibration | Dynamic Calibration |
|---|---|---|
| **Excitation** | Constant or quasi-static input | Time-varying (step, sine, ramp) |
| **Goal** | Sensitivity, offset, linearity | Bandwidth, damping, phase, step response |
| **Standards** | Dead-weight, piston gauge, precision voltage source | Shaker tables, pressure pulsers, shock tubes |
| **Outputs** | Transfer function $y = a_0 + a_1 x$ | Frequency response, settling time, ringing |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Compensation

Every MEMS device has **unwanted cross-sensitivities**. A capacitive pressure sensor also responds to:
- **Temperature** (gap and permittivity change).
- **Humidity** (permittivity of air changes by ~0.5 ppm/%RH).
- **Package stress** (die-attach induces ~0.1 % FSS error).
- **Ageing** (1–2 % drift over 10 years).

Compensation suppresses these so the **single desired measurand** dominates the output.

## 2.4 Hardware vs Software Compensation

| Feature | Hardware Compensation | Software Compensation |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | On-chip resistors, diodes, thermistors, switched-capacitor networks | Microcontroller / DSP polynomial evaluation, lookup tables |
| **Flexibility** | Low (fixed at design) | High (updatable, multi-point) |
| **Cost** | Adds die area | Costs only processing time |
| **Typical use** | First-order temperature trim | Higher-order cross-sensitivity correction |
| **KTU emphasis** | Analog trim networks, bipolar TCO cancellation | Polynomial fitting, Look-up Tables (LUT) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet for Numerical Problems — Memorize Before Exams**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear model | $y_{\text{out}} = a_0 + a_1 x$ | $a_0$ = offset, $a_1$ = sensitivity |
| 2 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | V/Pa, V/g, pF/μm |
| 3 | Two-point gain correction | $G = \dfrac{y_{2,\text{ref}} - y_{1,\text{ref}}}{y_{2,\text{m}} - y_{1,\text{m}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Two-point offset correction | $O = y_{1,\text{ref}} - G \cdot y_{1,\text{m}}$ | Same as $y$ |
| 5 | Corrected output | $y_{\text{corr}} = G \cdot y_{\text{m}} + O$ | — |
| 6 | % Non-Linearity | $NL\% = \dfrac{\max \vert y_{\text{meas}} - y_{\text{fit}} \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | **Use `\vert` not \vert in table — use $|$ inside math only** |
| 7 | % Hysteresis | $H\% = \dfrac{\max \vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow} \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | measured at same input |
| 8 | % Repeatability | $R\% = \dfrac{k \cdot \sigma}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | $k = 2$ for 95 % confidence |
| 9 | TCO | $\text{TCO} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{offset}}{\Delta T}$ | μV/°C or % FSS/°C |
| 10 | TCS | $\text{TCS} = \dfrac{1}{S}\dfrac{\Delta S}{\Delta T}$ | ppm/°C |
| 11 | 1st-order temperature compensation | $y_{\text{comp}} = y_{\text{raw}} - \text{TCO} \cdot (T - T_{\text{ref}})$ | Linear trim |
| 12 | Full multi-variable polynomial | $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 T + a_3 xT + a_4 T^2$ | Used in IMUs |
| 13 | Least-squares slope | $a_1 = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | Linear fit |
| 14 | Least-squares intercept | $a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$ | — |
| 15 | Bandwidth (–3 dB) | $f_{-3\text{dB}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{eq}} C_{\text{eq}}}$ | Dynamic spec |
| 16 | Allan variance noise floor | $\sigma_y(\tau) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2(N-1)} \sum (y_{i+1} - y_i)^2}$ | Inertial sensors |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU numericals, the most common question is the **two-point calibration problem** (Formulas 3, 4, 5) — practice 4–5 such problems before the exam.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive airbag accelerometers** require on-chip compensation for 0.1 g accuracy over –40 °C to +125 °C.
- **MEMS barometers in smartphones** use factory calibration + temperature compensation to achieve ±1 hPa.
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** in drones use 6-position (gravity-flip) calibration for hard-iron bias removal.
- **Hearing-aid microphones** need very low TCO (~±1 dB from 0–50 °C).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Two-Point Calibration Correction

A raw sensor reading $y_m$ is biased by a **gain error** $G_e$ and **offset error** $O_e$:

$$
y_m = (1 + G_e)\, y_{\text{true}} + O_e
$$

The **corrected** value must equal the true input $y_{\text{true}}$, so we apply a multiplicative gain $G$ and additive offset $O$:

$$
y_{\text{corr}} = G \cdot y_m + O
$$

Substituting and equating to $y_{\text{true}}$:

$$
G \cdot \bigl[(1 + G_e) y_{\text{true}} + O_e\bigr] + O \;=\; y_{\text{true}}
$$

Matching coefficients of $y_{\text{true}}$ and constants:

$$
G(1 + G_e) = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; G = \frac{1}{1 + G_e}
$$

$$
G \cdot O_e + O = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; O = -G \cdot O_e = -\frac{O_e}{1 + G_e}
$$

Now impose **two reference points** $(x_1, y_{1,\text{ref}})$ and $(x_2, y_{2,\text{ref}})$ with measured raw outputs $y_{1,m}$ and $y_{2,m}$:

$$
G = \frac{y_{2,\text{ref}} - y_{1,\text{ref}}}{y_{2,m} - y_{1,m}}
$$

$$
O = y_{1,\text{ref}} - G \cdot y_{1,m}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor is excited at two calibration points. Reference and measured values:

| Input $x$ (kPa) | $y_{\text{ref}}$ (mV) | $y_m$ (mV) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 12.5 |
| 100 | 80.0 | 96.4 |

Apply the formulas:

$$
G = \frac{80.0 - 0.0}{96.4 - 12.5} = \frac{80.0}{83.9} = 0.9535
$$

$$
O = 0.0 - 0.9535 \times 12.5 = -11.919 \text{ mV}
$$

Thus the corrected output equation is:

$$
y_{\text{corr}} = 0.9535 \cdot y_m - 11.919 \text{ mV}
$$

**Verification at $x = 100$ kPa:**

$$
y_{\text{corr}} = 0.9535 \times 96.4 - 11.919 = 91.92 - 11.919 = 80.0 \text{ mV} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Verification at $x = 0$ kPa:**

$$
y_{\text{corr}} = 0.9535 \times 12.5 - 11.919 = 11.919 - 11.919 = 0.0 \text{ mV} \;\;\checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Least-Squares Linear Fit

Given $n$ data pairs $(x_i, y_i)$ from a calibration sweep, the model is $\hat{y}_i = a_0 + a_1 x_i$. We minimize the sum of squared residuals:

$$
S(a_0, a_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl(y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i\bigr)^2
$$

Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_0} = -2 \sum (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_1} = -2 \sum x_i (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i) = 0
$$

These yield the **normal equations**:

$$
n a_0 + a_1 \sum x_i = \sum y_i
$$

$$
a_0 \sum x_i + a_1 \sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i
$$

Solving by Cramer's rule gives the **closed-form** slope and intercept already shown in the formula sheet (Rows 13, 14).

## 3.3 Derivation: First-Order Temperature Compensation

The raw output has a temperature-dependent bias:

$$
y_{\text{raw}}(T) = y_{\text{true}} + \text{TCO} \cdot (T - T_{\text{ref}})
$$

Subtracting the bias:

$$
y_{\text{comp}} = y_{\text{raw}} - \text{TCO} \cdot (T - T_{\text{ref}})
$$

If the sensitivity itself drifts:

$$
y_{\text{comp}} = \frac{y_{\text{raw}} - \text{TCO} \cdot (T - T_{\text{ref}})}{1 + \text{TCS} \cdot (T - T_{\text{ref}})}
$$

**Worked Example:** A capacitive accelerometer has TCO = 0.5 mg/°C and TCS = –50 ppm/°C. At $T = 60$ °C and $T_{\text{ref}} = 25$ °C:

$$
\Delta T = 60 - 25 = 35 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
y_{\text{comp}} = \frac{y_{\text{raw}} - 0.5 \times 35}{1 + (-50 \times 10^{-6}) \times 35}
$$

$$
y_{\text{comp}} = \frac{y_{\text{raw}} - 17.5 \text{ mg}}{1 - 1.75 \times 10^{-3}} \approx \frac{y_{\text{raw}} - 17.5}{0.99825}
$$

This shows that the **offset term (17.5 mg)** dominates over the **gain correction (0.175 %)** for moderate temperature ranges — a key KTU design insight.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Compensation Strategy

| Error Source | Magnitude (typical) | Best Compensation Strategy | On-Chip? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Offset drift vs T** | ±1–5 % FSS | Active biasing with temp. sensor | Yes |
| **Sensitivity drift vs T** | ±0.1–1 % FSS | Programmable gain amplifier (PGA) trim | Yes |
| **Package stress** | ±0.1–0.5 % FSS | Mechanical stress-relief + software LUT | Hybrid |
| **Non-linearity** | ±0.5–2 % FSS | 3rd-order polynomial fit in firmware | Software |
| **Cross-axis sensitivity** | ±1–5 % | Cross-axis matrix in IMU | Software |
| **Aging / long-term drift** | ±0.5–2 % / 10 yr | Periodic re-calibration | Field service |
| **Humidity** | ~0.1 % FSS / 50 %RH | Hermetic package + RH sensor LUT | Hybrid |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Calibration & Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSE[Physical Measurand] --> TRD[MEMS Transducer]
    TRD --> SCND[Signal Conditioning]
    SCND --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> CORE[Compensation Engine]
    TEMP[On-Chip Temp Sensor] --> CORE
    LUT[Non-Volatile LUT] --> CORE
    POLY[Polynomial Coefficients] --> CORE
    CORE --> CALC[Corrected Digital Output]
    FACT[Factory Calibration Station] -.Reference Standards.-> LUT
```

## 4.2 Two-Point Calibration Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start Calibration] --> apply0[Apply x = x1 Reference]
    apply0 --> read0[Record y1m from Sensor]
    read0 --> apply1[Apply x = x2 Reference]
    apply1 --> read1[Record y2m from Sensor]
    read1 --> compG[Compute Gain G]
    compG --> compO[Compute Offset O]
    compO --> store[Store G and O in Memory]
    store --> verify[Verify with x3 Independent Point]
    verify --> decision{Within Tolerance?}
    decision -- Yes --> pass[Pass - Use in Field]
    decision -- No --> reject[Reject or Re-Fit]
    pass --> end1[End]
    reject --> end1
```

## 4.3 Compensation Architecture (Block-Level Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage[Input Stage]
        A1[Pressure Sensor] --> A2[Charge Amplifier]
        B1[Temperature Sensor] --> B2[ADC]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingStage[DSP Stage]
        A2 --> A3[ADC]
        A3 --> P1[Offset Trim]
        B2 --> P1
        P1 --> P2[Gain Temperature Compensation]
        P2 --> P3[Cross-Sensitivity Matrix]
        P3 --> P4[Polynomial Linearization]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage[Output]
        P4 --> OUT[Calibrated Pressure in Pa]
    end
```

## 4.4 Drift & Error Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ERR[Total Sensor Error] --> S1[Systematic Errors]
    ERR --> S2[Random Errors]
    S1 --> Q1[Offset]
    S1 --> Q2[Sensitivity]
    S1 --> Q3[Non-Linearity]
    S1 --> Q4[Cross-Sensitivity]
    S2 --> R1[Noise]
    S2 --> R2[Repeatability]
    S2 --> R3[Drift over Time]
    Q1 --> CM1[Hardware Trim or LUT]
    Q2 --> CM2[PGA or Software Gain]
    Q3 --> CM3[Polynomial Fit]
    Q4 --> CM4[Matrix Calibration]
    R1 --> CM5[Filtering or Oversampling]
    R2 --> CM6[Statistical Averaging]
    R3 --> CM7[Periodic Re-Calibration]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**Differentiate between calibration and compensation in MEMS systems. State one commercial MEMS sensor where both are mandatory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Calibration | Compensation |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Quantify errors vs a known standard | Apply corrections to remove errors |
| **Stage** | Done at factory or in the field | Real-time, in device firmware/hardware |
| **Output** | Calibration coefficients (gain, offset, polynomial) | Corrected reading in SI units |
| **Tool** | Reference standards, test fixtures | On-chip DSP, LUTs, analog trim |

> A commercial example: **Bosch BMP388 barometric pressure sensor** — factory-calibrated gain/offset stored in OTP, with on-chip temperature compensation polynomial.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 for distinction, 1 for example.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
**Define the Temperature Coefficient of Offset (TCO). Why is TCO more critical than TCS in many MEMS pressure sensors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **TCO** is the change in the sensor's zero-pressure output per unit change in temperature, typically expressed in **% FSS/°C** or **μV/°C**.
>
> TCO is often more critical than TCS because:
> 1. **Offset adds directly** to every reading (it is *additive* error), while TCS is *multiplicative* and only matters far from zero.
> 2. **Pressure sensors near zero pressure** (barometric, medical low-pressure) suffer more from offset drift than gain drift.
> 3. **TCO of piezoresistive bridges** can be 1000+ ppm/°C — typically an order of magnitude higher than TCS.
>
> Hence, first-order TCO cancellation (using a half-active bridge or on-chip thermistor) is the **primary compensation step** in industrial MEMS pressure sensors.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** 
> - Do **not** skip the **units** in numerical problems (½ mark deducted per missing unit).
> - Always state the **assumptions** (e.g., linear model, no cross-sensitivity) at the start of the solution (1 mark reserved).
> - For 7-mark sub-parts, the final **boxed answer** must match the KTU answer key; **working alone is not enough**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]**
**A MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor has the following calibration data obtained at $T = 25$ °C:**

| Input Pressure (kPa) | Output (mV) |
|---|---|
| 0  | 5.0  |
| 25 | 23.8 |
| 50 | 42.6 |
| 75 | 61.4 |
| 100| 80.2 |

**Compute (i) the sensitivity using least-squares fit, (ii) the offset, and (iii) the % non-linearity at 50 kPa.**

**Model Solution:**

Compute the required summations (n = 5):

$$
\sum x_i = 0 + 25 + 50 + 75 + 100 = 250
$$

$$
\sum y_i = 5.0 + 23.8 + 42.6 + 61.4 + 80.2 = 213.0
$$

$$
\sum x_i^2 = 0 + 625 + 2500 + 5625 + 10000 = 18750
$$

$$
\sum x_i y_i = 0 + 595 + 2130 + 4605 + 8020 = 15350
$$

Apply the least-squares slope formula:

$$
a_1 = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}
$$

$$
a_1 = \frac{5 \times 15350 - 250 \times 213.0}{5 \times 18750 - (250)^2} = \frac{76750 - 53250}{93750 - 62500} = \frac{23500}{31250}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_1 = 0.752 \text{ mV/kPa}}
$$

Means:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{250}{5} = 50, \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{213.0}{5} = 42.6
$$

Intercept (offset):

$$
a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x} = 42.6 - 0.752 \times 50 = 42.6 - 37.6
$$

$$
\boxed{a_0 = 5.0 \text{ mV}}
$$

Best-fit model: $y_{\text{fit}} = 5.0 + 0.752 x$.

**% Non-linearity at 50 kPa:**

- $y_{\text{fit}}(50) = 5.0 + 0.752 \times 50 = 42.6$ mV (same as measured by coincidence).
- Compute $y_{\text{fit}}$ at all other points and find max deviation:

| $x$ (kPa) | $y_{\text{meas}}$ | $y_{\text{fit}} = 5.0 + 0.752x$ | Deviation (mV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0   | 5.0  | 5.00  | 0.00  |
| 25  | 23.8 | 23.80 | 0.00  |
| 50  | 42.6 | 42.60 | 0.00  |
| 75  | 61.4 | 61.40 | 0.00  |
| 100 | 80.2 | 80.20 | 0.00  |

The data is **perfectly linear** in this case, so:

$$
\boxed{NL\% = 0.0 \% \text{ FSS}}
$$

(If, in a real exam, the data were slightly non-linear, you would compute $\max \vert y_{\text{meas}} - y_{\text{fit}} \vert$, divide by $y_{\text{FS}} = 80.2$ mV, and multiply by 100.)

#### **(b) [7 Marks]**
**The same sensor is re-tested at $T = 60$ °C with $P = 0$ kPa. The output drifts to 9.5 mV. Calculate the TCO. If a thermistor-based bias resistor is added to cancel 80 % of this drift, what is the residual offset and the corresponding FSS error?**

**Model Solution:**

TCO formula:

$$
\text{TCO} = \frac{\Delta \text{offset}}{\Delta T} = \frac{9.5 - 5.0}{60 - 25} = \frac{4.5}{35}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TCO} = 0.1286 \text{ mV/°C}}
$$

(Alternatively, as % FSS/°C: $\text{TCO} = \dfrac{4.5}{80.2} \times \dfrac{1}{35} \times 100 = 0.160 \text{ % FSS/°C}$.)

If an external thermistor network compensates 80 % of the drift:

$$
\Delta V_{\text{comp}} = 0.80 \times 4.5 = 3.6 \text{ mV}
$$

Residual offset error at 60 °C:

$$
\Delta V_{\text{resid}} = 4.5 - 3.6 = 0.9 \text{ mV}
$$

Expressed as % FSS:

$$
\boxed{\text{Residual Error} = \frac{0.9}{80.2} \times 100 = 1.12 \text{ % FSS}}
$$

This shows that even a simple 80 % trim dramatically reduces the error from **5.6 % FSS** (uncompensated) to **1.12 % FSS** — the practical justification for on-chip TCO compensation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### **(a) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the six-position (gravity-flip) calibration procedure for a MEMS accelerometer used in an IMU. Justify why 6 positions are needed, not 4.**

**Model Solution:**

The accelerometer measures proper acceleration along its sensitive axis. By placing the sensor in **6 distinct orientations** (e.g., $\pm X$, $\pm Y$, $\pm Z$ with respect to gravity $g$), the measured output at each position is:

$$
a_{x,+} = +g + b_x, \qquad a_{x,-} = -g + b_x
$$

where $b_x$ is the bias. Averaging and differencing:

$$
\hat{g} = \frac{a_{x,+} - a_{x,-}}{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{scale-factor estimate}
$$

$$
\hat{b}_x = \frac{a_{x,+} + a_{x,-}}{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{bias estimate}
$$

**Why 6 positions and not 4:**

1. **Three axes** must each see both $+g$ and $-g$ to decouple the bias from the gain (a single sign per axis leaves the bias coupled to the scale factor).
2. **Cross-axis coupling** ($m_{xy}$, $m_{xz}$, …) is captured because each off-axis position produces a unique projection of gravity onto the other axes.
3. **Minimum deterministic solution:** Six equations (one per measured component) uniquely solve six unknowns (3 biases + 3 scale factors). A 4-position scheme is **underdetermined** and leaves an unobservable bias–gain combination.
4. The **$Z$-axis pair** is the only reliable means to separate vertical cross-coupling from the $X$ and $Y$ biases.

Thus the 6-position scheme is the **minimum observable configuration** for full bias + scale-factor identification.

#### **(b) [7 Marks]**
**A 3-axis MEMS gyroscope used in a smartphone IMU has the following factory trim coefficients stored in OTP:**

$$
K_x = 1.002, \quad K_y = 0.998, \quad K_z = 1.005
$$

$$
B_x = +0.3 °/\text{s}, \quad B_y = -0.2 °/\text{s}, \quad B_z = +0.1 °/\text{s}
$$

**The raw readings at one instant are: $\omega_{x,\text{raw}} = 10.5$ °/s, $\omega_{y,\text{raw}} = -45.2$ °/s, $\omega_{z,\text{raw}} = 25.1$ °/s. Compute the compensated angular rate vector.**

**Model Solution:**

The general compensation model is:

$$
\omega_{\text{comp}} = K \cdot \omega_{\text{raw}} - B
$$

For each axis:

$$
\omega_{x,\text{comp}} = 1.002 \times 10.5 - 0.3 = 10.521 - 0.3 = 10.221 \text{ °/s}
$$

$$
\omega_{y,\text{comp}} = 0.998 \times (-45.2) - (-0.2) = -45.1096 + 0.2 = -44.9096 \text{ °/s}
$$

$$
\omega_{z,\text{comp}} = 1.005 \times 25.1 - 0.1 = 25.2255 - 0.1 = 25.1255 \text{ °/s}
$$

**Compensated angular rate vector:**

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\omega}_{\text{comp}} = \bigl[\,10.221,\;\; -44.910,\;\; 25.126\,\bigr] \text{ °/s}}
$$

**Significance:** Without compensation, the bias alone would falsely indicate a rotation of 0.3 °/s about $X$ even when the phone is perfectly still — a critical error in dead-reckoning and AR/VR applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read 30 Minutes Before the Exam**

- **Calibration** = determining sensor errors vs. a *traceable standard*; **Compensation** = applying corrections in *real time*.
- **Two-point calibration** gives gain $G$ and offset $O$; the corrected output is $y_{\text{corr}} = G y_m + O$.
- **Least-squares fit** is used when more than 2 reference points are available; always use the closed-form normal equations.
- **Static metrics:** sensitivity, offset, % NL, % hysteresis, % repeatability, TCO, TCS.
- **TCO** is more critical than TCS for low-pressure / near-zero measurements because it is *additive*.
- **Hardware compensation** is fast but inflexible (resistor / diode networks, switched-cap).
- **Software compensation** is flexible (LUTs, polynomials) but adds latency and processing power.
- **6-position (gravity-flip)** is the *minimum observable* calibration for IMU accelerometers.
- **Gyroscope compensation** = $K \cdot \omega_{\text{raw}} - B$ for each axis, with cross-axis matrix for full IMU calibration.
- **Temperature compensation** ranges from first-order TCO trim to multi-variable polynomial $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 T + a_3 xT + a_4 T^2$.
- **MEMS-specific drift sources:** residual stress, die-attach stress, aging of piezoresistors, humidity ingress.
- **Always state assumptions** in numerical answers (linearity, no cross-sensitivity, etc.) — KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for this.
- **Real-world examples to quote:** Bosch BMP388 (barometer), ST LSM6DSO (6-axis IMU), Analog Devices ADXL345 (accelerometer), InvenSense ICM-42688 (IMU).
- **In formulas, prefer `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value** to avoid KTU's automated LaTeX parser breaking.
- **Common mistake:** confusing *gain error* (multiplicative) with *offset error* (additive) — write the model $y_m = (1+G_e) y_{\text{true}} + O_e$ explicitly when you derive anything.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Die Attach Process: Solder Alloy and Bonding Process Using Solder Alloy
Organic Adhesive Wiring and Interconnections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT744 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Organic Adhesive Wiring and Interconnections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Organic Adhesive Wiring and Interconnections** refers to a class of low-temperature, polymer-based electrical and mechanical joining techniques used in MEMS packaging, where organic resins (epoxies, acrylates, polyimides, silicones) loaded with conductive fillers (metal particles, carbon nanotubes, silver flakes) or used in conjunction with conductive films, establish electrical pathways and mechanical anchoring between a MEMS die and its substrate/host system.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for MEMS (PEMRT744), this topic bridges **Module 4 (Electronic Interfaces & Packaging)** — addressing how the fragile micro-scale mechanical structure of a MEMS device is electrically and mechanically linked to the macro-scale printed circuit board (PCB) or carrier, without damaging temperature-sensitive components, microstructures, or released suspended parts.

The four primary families of organic adhesive interconnections discussed in KTU syllabus are:

| # | Family | Abbreviation | Conductivity Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Isotropic Conductive Adhesive | ICA | Omni-directional (x, y, z) |
| 2 | Anisotropic Conductive Film | ACF | Z-direction only |
| 3 | Anisotropic Conductive Paste | ACP | Z-direction only |
| 4 | Non-Conductive Paste / Film | NCP / NCF | Mechanical only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Organic"?** The term distinguishes these from *inorganic* solders (Pb-Sn, SAC alloys) and metallic brazes. The binder matrix is an organic polymer that cures (cross-links) at low temperatures (**80 °C – 200 °C**), making them suitable for MEMS where released structures, CMOS wafers, and polymer substrates cannot tolerate reflow temperatures of **>250 °C**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Conductive Glue Stick" Analogy

Imagine you are assembling a delicate, hand-made decorative lamp whose glass shades will **crack or melt** if you use a high-temperature torch (analogous to lead-tin soldering). Instead, you use a **specially formulated glue** (the *organic adhesive*) in which tiny silver specks are suspended.

- When you press the glue between two metal pads and let it harden, the silver specks trapped between the two pads line up vertically and **conduct electricity only in the pressing direction (Z-axis)** — just like the lined-up pencils in a jar.
- Sideways (X-Y), the specks do **not** touch, so no current leaks to neighboring wires.

This is precisely how an **Anisotropic Conductive Film (ACF)** behaves during flip-chip bonding of a MEMS die.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Organic Adhesive = Low-Temperature Conductive "Glue"* for fragile MEMS.

---

## 1.3 Why Organic Adhesives in MEMS?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Point:** Always mention **three** driving factors when answering "Why organic adhesives in MEMS?"
> 1. **Low process temperature** (typically **<200 °C**) — protects released MEMS structures, CMOS integrated circuitry, and flexible polymer substrates.
> 2. **Fine-pitch capability** — modern ACFs achieve **<40 µm pitch**, critical for MEMS sensor arrays (e.g., image sensors, accelerometer ASIC interfaces).
> 3. **Compliance & stress relief** — cured polymer absorbs **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** mismatch stresses between silicon die (~**2.6 ppm/°C**) and PCB/FR-4 (~**15–17 ppm/°C**).

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold for Memory)

- **Standard ACF bonding temperature:** 180 °C – 220 °C
- **Bonding pressure:** 30 MPa – 100 MPa (localized on bumps)
- **Conductive filler size:** 3 µm – 10 µm (Ni, Ag, Au-coated polymer spheres)
- **Filler loading by volume:** 5 % – 20 % (for ACF); 30 % – 40 % (for ICA)
- **Percolation threshold (volume fraction):** ϕ_c ≈ **0.30 – 0.35** for spherical fillers
- **Bulk resistivity of cured ICA:** ~**10⁻⁴ Ω·cm** (close to eutectic solder)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Anatomy of an Organic Adhesive Joint

A complete organic adhesive interconnection is a multi-phase composite. Let us deconstruct it layer-by-layer (from substrate upward):

1. **Substrate Pad** (Cu/Au on PCB or glass)
2. **Adhesive Matrix (Binder):** Thermosetting polymer — typically **epoxy**, **acrylic**, or **polyurethane**. Provides mechanical hold.
3. **Conductive Filler Particles:** Metallic spheres (Ni, Ag, Au/polymer) — provide electrical pathways.
4. **Die Pad / Bump:** Au-stud, Cu pillar, or ACF-compatible metallization on MEMS die.
5. **Cured Cross-linked Network:** Formed during thermo-compression bonding; locks fillers in place.

> [!NOTE]
> The **thermo-compression bonding** step (simultaneous application of **heat + pressure + time**) is what transforms a soft adhesive film into a hardened, conducting joint. Each parameter is critical:
> - **Heat** → activates the cross-linking reaction (curing).
> - **Pressure** → deforms the filler spheres between the two pads, creating a low-resistance metal-to-metal contact.
> - **Time** → ensures full polymerization (cure).

---

## 2.2 The Percolation Theory Foundation (The "Why" Behind Conductivity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked:** *"Why does increasing filler loading suddenly make the adhesive conductive?"* → Answer lies in **Percolation Theory**.

In a polymer-filler composite, conductivity is essentially zero until the filler volume fraction (ϕ) crosses a critical threshold called the **percolation threshold (ϕ_c)**. Below ϕ_c, particles are isolated; above ϕ_c, a continuous chain of touching particles spans the joint.

The classical scaling law for conductivity near the percolation threshold:

$$
\sigma \;\propto\; \left( \phi - \phi_c \right)^{t}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = effective electrical conductivity of the composite (S/m)
- $\phi$ = volume fraction of conductive filler (dimensionless, 0–1)
- $\phi_c$ = percolation threshold (typically **0.30 – 0.35** for randomly packed spheres)
- $t$ = critical exponent, experimentally **≈ 1.65 – 2.0** in 3D

For **ICA** design, we choose ϕ well above ϕ_c (e.g., **0.35 – 0.40**). For **ACF**, we deliberately stay below ϕ_c in the bulk film — conductivity arises only at compressed bump sites where local ϕ exceeds ϕ_c.

---

## 2.3 Contact Resistance at a Single ACF Bump

When an ACF joint is formed, current flows through a chain:

**Pad → Deformed particle → Matrix → Deformed particle → Pad**

The total joint contact resistance $R_j$ has three additive components:

$$
R_j \;=\; R_{pad1} \;+\; R_{constriction} \;+\; R_{pad2}
$$

The **constriction (spreading) resistance** dominates and is given by the Holm formula for a single circular contact spot of radius $a$:

$$
R_{constriction} \;=\; \frac{\rho}{4a}
$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = resistivity of the filler particle material (e.g., Ni ≈ **6.9 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m**, Au ≈ **2.2 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m**)
- $a$ = effective contact radius after deformation (typically **1 – 3 µm** for a 5 µm sphere flattened under load)

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** KTU examiners often ask for the *order-of-magnitude* joint resistance. A well-formed ACF joint shows **5 – 20 mΩ per bump** — comparable to solder joints.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All critical formulas in one place — revise before every exam.**

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma \propto (\phi - \phi_c)^t$ | $\sigma$ = composite conductivity, $\phi$ = filler volume fraction, $\phi_c$ ≈ 0.30, $t$ ≈ 1.8 | Justifying why ICA needs high filler loading |
| 2 | $R_j = R_{pad1} + R_{constriction} + R_{pad2}$ | Total ACF/ICA joint resistance | ACF joint resistance estimation |
| 3 | $R_{constriction} = \rho / (4a)$ | $\rho$ = filler resistivity, $a$ = contact spot radius | Single-bump contact resistance |
| 4 | $V_{binder} + V_{filler} = 1$ | Volume conservation in composite | Compute filler mass loading |
| 5 | $\Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L = \delta_{CTE}$ | $\Delta\alpha$ = CTE mismatch, $L$ = die dimension, $\Delta T$ = thermal swing | CTE-induced shear strain |
| 6 | $P_{bond} = F / A_{bump}$ | Bonding pressure = force / bump area | Selecting thermode pressure |
| 7 | $t_{cure} = A \cdot e^{E_a / (RT)}$ (Arrhenius) | Cure kinetics; $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = gas constant | Estimating cure time at lower temperature |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Organic Adhesives? |
|---|---|
| **MEMS Accelerometer / Gyroscope ASIC bonding** | Low temperature protects the released proof mass; ACF handles fine pitch |
| **CMOS Image Sensor (CIS) packaging** | Replaces expensive solder bump reflow; ACF eliminates flux residue on optics |
| **Flexible / Wearable Electronics** | Polymer substrates (PI, PET) cannot survive solder reflow |
| **Display Driver ICs (LCD/OLED COG)** | Chip-on-Glass using ACF is industry-standard |
| **RFID Antenna Interconnects** | Low-cost, printable conductive epoxy inks |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Percolation-Based Conductivity in ICA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why derive this?** KTU 2024 examiners reward derivations that show the **transition from random to continuous conductive paths**.

### Given:
A composite is made of monodisperse silver spheres (radius $r = 2.5 \,\mu m$) dispersed uniformly in epoxy resin. The volume fraction of filler is $\phi = 0.35$. The percolation threshold for random close packing of spheres is $\phi_c = 0.32$. The critical exponent is $t = 1.8$.

### To Find:
Conductivity ratio $\sigma / \sigma_{metal}$ where $\sigma_{Ag} = 6.3 \times 10^7 \, S/m$.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 —** Identify the distance from percolation threshold:

$$
\Delta\phi \;=\; \phi - \phi_c \;=\; 0.35 - 0.32 \;=\; 0.03
$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the scaling factor:

$$
(\Delta\phi)^t \;=\; (0.03)^{1.8}
$$

We evaluate this using logarithms:

$$
\log_{10}(0.03) = \log_{10}(3 \times 10^{-2}) = 0.4771 - 2 = -1.5229
$$

$$
\log_{10}\bigl[(0.03)^{1.8}\bigr] = 1.8 \times (-1.5229) = -2.7412
$$

$$
(0.03)^{1.8} = 10^{-2.7412} \approx 1.815 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 —** Apply the scaling law:

$$
\sigma \;=\; \sigma_{Ag} \cdot (\Delta\phi)^t
$$

$$
\sigma \;=\; (6.3 \times 10^{7}) \times (1.815 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
\sigma \;\approx\; 1.143 \times 10^{5} \, S/m
$$

**Step 4 —** Convert to resistivity for readability:

$$
\rho_{ICA} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sigma} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.143 \times 10^{5}} \;\approx\; 8.75 \times 10^{-6} \, \Omega\!\cdot\!m \;=\; 8.75 \times 10^{-4} \, \Omega\!\cdot\!cm
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Even though ϕ is only marginally above ϕ_c (Δϕ = 0.03), the conductivity jumps by **~5 orders of magnitude** compared to pure epoxy. This is the *signature* of percolation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: ACF Constriction Resistance per Bump

### Given:
- Filler particle = gold-coated nickel sphere, diameter $d = 5 \,\mu m$
- Particle resistivity = use nickel bulk: $\rho_{Ni} \approx 6.9 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega\!\cdot\!m$
- After thermode compression, the sphere is flattened to a contact spot of effective radius $a = 1.5 \,\mu m$

### Step 1 —** Write the Holm constriction equation:

$$
R_{constriction} \;=\; \frac{\rho}{4a}
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute numerical values:

$$
R_{constriction} \;=\; \frac{6.9 \times 10^{-8}}{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

**Step 3 —** Compute denominator:

$$
4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 6.0 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4 —** Final division:

$$
R_{constriction} \;=\; \frac{6.9 \times 10^{-8}}{6.0 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.15 \times 10^{-2} \, \Omega \;=\; 11.5 \, m\Omega
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A single well-formed ACF bump exhibits ~**11.5 mΩ**. Adding the two pad resistances (typically **3–5 mΩ each**), the total joint resistance $R_j \approx 18$–**20 mΩ** — comfortably within KTU's expected range.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CTE Mismatch Shear Strain at the Joint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Weight Derivation**

### Given:
- MEMS die (silicon): $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \, ppm/°C$
- PCB (FR-4): $\alpha_{PCB} = 16 \, ppm/°C$
- Die length: $L = 5 \, mm = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, m$
- Temperature swing: $\Delta T = -150 \, °C$ (e.g., from curing at 200 °C down to 50 °C operation)

### Step 1 —** Compute the differential expansion:

$$
\delta_{CTE} \;=\; (\alpha_{PCB} - \alpha_{Si}) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute:

$$
\delta_{CTE} \;=\; (16 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \times (-150) \times (5 \times 10^{-3})
$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate the bracket:

$$
(16 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 13.4 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 1.34 \times 10^{-5} \, /°C
$$

**Step 4 —** Multiply:

$$
\delta_{CTE} \;=\; 1.34 \times 10^{-5} \times (-150) \times 5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\delta_{CTE} \;=\; 1.34 \times 10^{-5} \times (-0.75)
$$

$$
\delta_{CTE} \;=\; -1.005 \times 10^{-5} \, m \;=\; -10.05 \, \mu m
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The PCB contracts more than the silicon die during cooldown, imposing a ~**10 µm shear displacement** at the joint. A compliant polymer adhesive (CTE ≈ **60–80 ppm/°C**) absorbs this via elastic deformation — preventing **delamination** and **fatigue cracking** seen with rigid solder joints.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ACF Flip-Chip Bonding Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MEMS Die with Au Bumps] --> B[Pre-bond: Align to Substrate Pads]
    B --> C[Place ACF Lamination on Substrate]
    C --> D[Pick up Die and Align with Camera Vision System]
    D --> E[Stage 1: Preliminary Tack at 80 C for 3 sec]
    E --> F[Stage 2: Main Bonding at 180-220 C with 50-100 MPa for 10-20 sec]
    F --> G[ACF Filler Particles Deform and Trap Between Bump and Pad]
    G --> H[Cure Completes - Polymer Crosslinks]
    H --> I[Z-direction Conduction Established via Deformed Particles]
    H --> J[X-Y Isolation Maintained - Sparse Filler Density]
    I --> K[Verified Joint with 10-20 mOhm Resistance]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final MEMS Package Ready for Encapsulation]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison: ICA vs ACF vs Solder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ICA[ICA - Isotropic Conductive Adhesive]
        ICA1[High Filler Loading 30-40 percent] --> ICA2[Conducts in all directions]
        ICA2 --> ICA3[Used for die attach and ground plane bonding]
    end

    subgraph ACF[ACF - Anisotropic Conductive Film]
        ACF1[Low Filler Loading 5-15 percent] --> ACF2[Conducts only at compressed Z-axis points]
        ACF2 --> ACF3[Used for fine pitch flip chip interconnect]
    end

    subgraph SAC[SAC Solder - Conventional Reference]
        SAC1[Metallic alloy reflow above 230 C] --> SAC2[Conducts everywhere it wets]
        SAC2 --> SAC3[NOT suitable for post released MEMS]
    end

    ICA3 --> COMPARE[Choose based on pitch, temperature budget, and MEMS release stage]
    ACF3 --> COMPARE
    SAC3 --> COMPARE
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single ACF Bump Cross-Section (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP[Top Electrode - Au Bump on Die]
        TOP1[Au Bump]
    end

    subgraph MID[Middle - Adhesive Matrix with Filler]
        MID1[Epoxy Binder Cured]
        MID2[Ni Particle 1 Compressed]
        MID3[Ni Particle 2 Compressed]
```
